insights.insight: Amporilla has many counters and won't win most sets, but completely dominates and outscales everything when it does win, making it a high-variance unit that feels oppressive ('salt unit') without being fundamentally overpowered.
insights.author: apooche
insights.context: Assessment of Amporilla's power level and playstyle impact.

insights.insight: Wall at 5B is intentionally 'imbalanced' (overpowered) by design because absorb mechanics are the core balance lever that prevents low-economy rushing from dominating every game; the entire game is balanced around base set, so base set rebalancing is unnecessary.
insights.author: steel0229e
insights.context: Discussion of whether base set units should be rebalanced; steel0229e and awaclus argue the base set is already perfectly balanced.

insights.insight: Redeemer + Thunderhead is one of the more degenerate combos in the game and is unmirrorable, making it difficult to counter with standard P1 openings.
insights.author: crash_overlord
insights.context: Discussion of a particularly problematic P2 combo.

insights.insight: Redeemer pre-nerf had very high Player 2 winrate (around 58% in pure Redeemer mirrors), which prompted the balance change; in mixed sets the advantage is lower as Player 1 can pursue alternative strategies.
insights.author: 307th, liadahlia
insights.context: Discussion of Player 1 vs Player 2 advantage in Redeemer-heavy sets.

insights.insight: Redeemer + Chieftain as a P2 strategy is extremely strong (msven estimates ~80% win rate in favourable contexts), but only constitutes an autowin if the +6 units provide no viable counterplay; the claim of autowin is often overstated by conflating the combo's strength with set frequency.
insights.author: msven, extratricky, mtanzer
insights.context: Debate over whether Redeemer + Chieftain is an autowin for P2.

insights.insight: Smorcus is a highly efficient attacker, nearly as strong as old Flame Animus Perforator combo (which was nerfed), with Animus click yielding roughly Omega Splitter efficiency or slightly better.
insights.author: .bky_1556
insights.context: Analysis of Smorcus power level and efficiency comparisons.

insights.insight: Smorcus is a strong pressure unit offering 5 red at cost 5RR with 1 threat, comparable in power to Gauss Cannon but with additional threat scaling; the unit's power is somewhat limited by Animus scarcity (most games have only 1 Animus).
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Unit balance assessment.

insights.insight: Lucina's nerf (reducing red output) is significant only in games where you have good red sinks and red soak available; if there are no red sinks, the nerf is largely irrelevant to her power level.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Analysis of how Lucina's balance change affects her viability.

insights.insight: Fusion Reactor was significantly nerfed (from 12EEE cost to 9 cost equivalent), making it rarely correct to purchase outside of specific Vai or Amporilla synergy contexts, where Chronofuge often performs the same role better.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Discussion of Fusion Reactor viability post-nerf and remaining niche uses.

insights.insight: New Thermite (post-2018 balance patch) is overpowered due to spawning 2 Pixies per activation, enabling absorb denial up to 9 attack without sacrificing versatility.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Extended discussion of Thermite balance and versatility.

insights.insight: Odin is extremely busted in sets containing Drake, Grenade Mech, Manticore, or Lancetooth, where first-player to secure Odin wins; however, it is usually balanced in other sets.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Discussion of problematic units that create unfair first-mover advantages.

insights.insight: Odin is 'fair and balanced' compared to Centurion, which is more overtuned. Odin does tend to cause player imbalance because one player (usually P1) usually has a better line to get it out, making it player-favored.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Comparison of Odin and Centurion balance, noting Odin's player-favoredness.

insights.insight: The Odin nerf (cost increase from 14BBB to 20BBB) was very large and now makes Odin worse than Omega, which was already considered weak; ideally Odin should cost around 14.5BBB to be more justifiable.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Expert assessment of Odin balance after recent patch.

insights.insight: Wild Drone is a problem in over 90% of sets where it appears because it forces early game plays to be very similar, removing meaningful decision-making (typically reduces opening options from 3 viable choices to 1).
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Conversation about how Wild Drone reduces player agency in deckbuilding and early-game strategy.

insights.insight: Big units tend to be more cost-efficient than small units because they are harder to obtain; if a plausible excuse to purchase a legendary exists, it is usually not a bad idea.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: General principle about unit efficiency scaling.

insights.insight: Cynestra is approximately the best attacker in the game due to efficiency and convenient GGGB cost (requiring fewer drones to generate the necessary Green).
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Expert ranking of advanced attackers.

insights.insight: Venge Cannon is extremely efficient without the click ability when you do not have to buy drones alongside it. At a barrier value of 2.4, it is slightly more efficient than Shadowfang.
insights.author: anesotericman
insights.context: Mathematical analysis of unit efficiency using barrier value and interest rate models.

insights.insight: Old Grenade Mech with 0 click cost would enable spam-clicking similar to Drake spam, but required building up economy to sustain Blastforge cycling. The 2-gold cost difference (Grenade Mech vs Drake) plus 1 extra damage per click (3 vs 2) made it significantly stronger than Drake spam strategies.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Historical discussion of Grenade Mech balance changes and why 0 click cost was overpowered.

insights.insight: Galvani Drone is severely overpowered in balanced event format. It should cost buildtime 2 instead of buildtime 1 to prevent using it as a drone replacement and enable absorb strategies.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Criticism of Galvani Drone's balance in event format.

insights.insight: Galvantima (Galvani Drone + Antima Comet) is overrated; rushing Antima against Shredder is not a good strategy.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Brief assessment of Galvantima unit combination viability.

insights.insight: In most sets, going for a random attacker should beat fast Tarsier spam; if you're new, memorizing Tarsier lines may be easier but the strategy is not inherently overpowered.
insights.author: Deleted User
insights.context: Discussion of why Tarsier spam appears unbeatable to new players.

insights.insight: Absorb denial (the mechanic where units like Tarsier prevent absorb with their build time) is identified as a highly powerful mechanic that significantly affects unit valuation.
insights.author: namington
insights.context: Comparative assessment of broken mechanics in Prismata.

insights.insight: Cryo Ray's value depends heavily on situation; as a freeze mechanic, it is generally weaker than Gauss Cannon because Gauss Cannon permanently removes blockers while Cryo Ray only temporarily freezes them, making Gauss Cannon superior for forcing breaches in most concrete scenarios.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Extended debate on Cryo Ray vs Gauss Cannon efficiency and tactical application.

insights.insight: In a set containing both Grimbotch and Amporilla, Grimbotch dominates and Amporilla becomes irrelevant to optimal play—this is presented as a general rule that has exceptions depending on broader set composition.
insights.author: crash_overlord (stated as general rule), challenged by context
insights.context: Discussion about whether Amporilla is ever viable when Grimbotch exists.

insights.insight: Grimbotch's value is primarily in small red efficiency; it outperforms large red units like Lucina in sets where early game pressure matters. Tech costs make big red units less efficient despite raw damage-per-cost numbers.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Comparison of red attacker efficiency across different game phases and set contexts.

insights.insight: Arms Race is a fundamentally weak card; it becomes even worse in Galvani Drone sets (Galvani provides defensive economy that competes with Arms Race value) or Electrovore sets, making it not worth considering in those contexts.
insights.author: awaclus
insights.context: Discussion of Arms Race viability in specific unit sets.

insights.insight: Arms Race is often undervalued by mathematical analysis that ignores context. It functions as a bigger, slower Tarsier, but should not be called 'total trash' because it is frequently bought in many pools and has strong long-term value, especially in sets where it forces early pressure.
insights.author: p0lari
insights.context: Debate about Arms Race's strength and why percentile-based rankings can mislead about unit viability.

insights.insight: Arka Sadora's prompt mechanic enables counter-arka gameplay which can lead to stalled positions where neither player builds first Arka; the design masks over-tuning in cost/stats, similar to how Immaculon was over-buffed with Plexo Cells.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Discussion of unit balance design philosophy and whether counter-play mechanics justify stat inflation.

insights.insight: Doomed Drone creates situations where early Player 1 Conduit openings (e.g., DD/11C) become viable, giving Player 1 a tempo boost. However, this does not necessarily flip P2-favored Conduit sets to P1-favored, as the tradeoff involves lower permanent economy for Player 1.
insights.author: amalloy, VanitasCabal
insights.context: Discussion of whether Doomed Drone changes Player 1 versus Player 2 matchups in Conduit sets.

insights.insight: Valkyrion is worth buying in a vacuum and is one of the best units in the game, but it is awkward to tech for. Without synergies, it is underwhelming (roughly equivalent to two Borehole Patrollers bundled together). With good synergies, it accelerates the game dramatically but few Valkyrianes can be built before game ends.
insights.author: liadahlia, crash_overlord, p0lari, vanitascabal6962
insights.context: Debate on Valkyrion's strength relative to its tech cost and synergy requirements.

insights.insight: Centurion is problematic because it dominates most sets it appears in and is almost always correct to buy, which some players dislike for reducing set diversity, though the unit may still be balanced if it doesn't favor one player over the other.
insights.author: namington, masn6811
insights.context: Discussion about whether certain units are 'imba' (imbalanced) by virtue of being mandatory purchases in most sets.

insights.insight: Centaurion with natural Animus is a likely 2-engineer econ unit for P1 in base+Centurion sets, as P1 wins consistently against any reasonable P2 line starting with Animus.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Inquiry about optimal econ level in Centurion sets.

insights.insight: Centurion is skippable less often than commonly assumed but is still correct to skip in approximately 7-10% of games. It is the correct purchase more than 90% of the time.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Meta assessment of Centurion's overall viability.

insights.insight: Centrifuge's post-nerf cost (3GG instead of 20GG) and effect creates a feeling of poor resource management when purchased, rather than enabling exciting game-swinging moments; this is psychologically different from the old version despite similar quantitative efficiency in niche cases.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Discussion of how Centrifuge's nerf affects player psychology and unit appeal.

insights.insight: Centrifuge (Fuge) has been significantly weakened over time and is now only reliably good with Wincer, whereas it used to enable Omega, Wincer, and Lucina builds in earlier patches.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Historical balance observation on Centrifuge decline

insights.insight: Deadeye Operative is strictly superior to Savior in passive Conduit sets because Savior's high cost leaves insufficient drones (roughly 9) to amortize its value before Deadeye supply exhaustion.
insights.author: xujhan, redzonex
insights.context: Discussion of attacker choice in specific set archetype.

insights.insight: Bloodrager has exceptionally high inflation even when denying only 2 absorb; it may warrant a nerf but does not lead to degenerate games in practice.
insights.author: namington
insights.context: Balance assessment of absorb-denial units and their impact.

insights.insight: Thorium Dynamo is very good but does not create degenerate gameplay despite high inflation; similar to Bloodrager in being overpowered without causing bad games.
insights.author: namington
insights.context: Balance discussion on units that are strong without warping game quality.

insights.insight: The heuristic 'base+Energy Matrix is 2 Engineer' is misleading because P2 is severely disadvantaged in green-only mirrors; P1 can successfully go third Engineer on this matchup even though base+Matrix suggests otherwise.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Critique of common opening theory heuristic.

insights.insight: Bombarder seems overly strong and is purchased automatically even without clear purpose, suggesting it may be overcosted for its power level.
insights.author: mqp
insights.context: Player discussing unit balance in early July 2018.

insights.insight: Buying both Manimus and Scorchilla is inefficient. Each is good for converting Red when you don't want it anymore—use one or the other, not both together.
insights.author: tarolg
insights.context: Critique of redundant unit combinations.

insights.insight: Hellhound is a strong attacker for ramping damage and scales well with Resonator as a granular attacker, but is not as efficient as Rager or Cynestra.
insights.author: terroking, coffeeyay
insights.context: Discussion of Hellhound's efficiency as an attacker in various opening strategies.

insights.insight: Feral Warden creates repetitive set dynamics, particularly in small red sets where P2 can smoothly balance defense and offense simultaneously, making many such sets feel similar.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Complaint about Feral Warden's overuse in similar set patterns.

insights.insight: Chrono Filter, not Antima Comet, should be blamed for the degeneracy of sets containing both units, as the frontloaded tech generation from Chrono enables the Antima combo to come online too quickly.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Discussion of which unit causes degenerate gameplay in Chrono/Galvani/Antima sets.

insights.insight: Apollo is underrated by the community; players claim it is weak but buy it frequently in practice, indicating it is a decent unit especially in slower sets with blue sinks.
insights.author: sano712, siepu
insights.context: Meta discussion of Apollo's perceived vs. actual strength.

insights.insight: Plasmafier is a very good attacker on par with Cynestra and Shadowfang in efficiency.
insights.author: megasupp
insights.context: Assessment of Plasmafier's standing among advanced attackers.

insights.insight: Plasmafier is typically considered a strong attacker because it doesn't require sacrificing drones as fuel, while Redeemer (donger) is considered below average but becomes stronger when strong soak units are available.
insights.author: cooledcannon, velizar_
insights.context: Relative unit strength assessment for red attackers.

insights.insight: Tantalum Ray appears overrated by 33% interest rate; it should be valued at 40% interest rate or higher (around 3 clicks in typical scenarios) rather than its naive value. Asteroid (Asteri) is notably more efficient than Tantalum Ray (~5 clicks vs ~3 clicks).
insights.author: mtanzer, xnor_gate, jamberine
insights.context: Comparison of relative efficiency of two red-based attackers at different interest rates.

insights.insight: Antima Comet is too efficient and forces uncertain decisions between buying attack and buying defense. Changing cost to 2GBB or 9GBBR with buildtime 3 would increase commitment and decision clarity.
insights.author: masn6811, silentslayers
insights.context: Balance discussion of Antima Comet cost and commit

insights.insight: Savior needs a commitment cost (like Resophore treatment) to force players to play real games before it arrives. Alternative: make Savior produce Infusions or Stamina units instead of Plasma, or make Plasma units Prompt.
insights.author: masn6811, pikachumemes, megasupp
insights.context: Design suggestions for Savior balance.

insights.insight: Rhino is undervalued relative to its cost; it's worth approximately 5 gold, possibly overpriced compared to Grimbotch which costs 5 and attacks one extra time.
insights.author: velizar_, shadourow
insights.context: Players evaluating unit value in context of Mobile Animus + Vai conversion strategy.

insights.insight: Gauss Cannon is primarily effective in breachproof strategies; some players argue its supply should be increased to make breachproof more viable, while others contend breachproof is appropriately balanced by being rarely viable.
insights.author: pikachumemes, awaclus
insights.context: Debate over breachproof viability and balance of Gauss Cannon supply.

insights.insight: "GF'd" (Greatly Favored) means the opponent has a significant advantage that approaches an autowin; players often overstate winrate implications when using this term.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Definition of common Prismata community term in replay analysis discussion.

insights.insight: Base set units are commonly abbreviated by their in-game hotkey: F = Forcefield (not FF which is ambiguous for two Forcefields); this notation is standard in replays and analysis.
insights.author: p0lari, steel0229e
insights.context: Clarification of unit abbreviation convention to avoid confusion.

insights.insight: In Prismata notation, lowercase letters (r, g, b) denote resource types (red, green, blue) and uppercase letters (R, G, B) denote units that produce those resources. Gold is typically written with a standalone number (e.g., '2' means 2 gold), and can optionally be labeled as '2 gold' for clarity to new players. Capital G is reserved for Gaussite.
insights.author: mrguy888, apooche
insights.context: Discussion of unit cost notation and standardization for new player education.

insights.insight: 'Splashing a color' means buying a small amount of one color in a build dominated by another color (e.g., buying one Red unit in a mostly Green economy).
insights.author: miuna6933
insights.context: Definition of strategic terminology used in set analysis.

insights.insight: Sets are classified as 'tactical' when they feature aggressive attackers and low efficient defense, putting emphasis on exact health exchanges and narrow decision windows.
insights.author: namington
insights.context: Definition of meta-language used in set analysis and strategy discussion.

insights.insight: 'Timewalking' refers to invalidating your opponent's entire turn or action for minimal cost, essentially giving yourself an extra turn. Borrowed from Magic: The Gathering terminology.
insights.author: swapgo, cogito3
insights.context: Definition of common strategic terminology.

insights.insight: The term 'permanent attack' has fallen out of favor in Prismata strategy discussion because it is not a particularly useful metric for comparing positions.
insights.author: apooche
insights.context: Historical discussion of evaluation terminology in strategy.

insights.insight: Shock: a unit property describing units that threaten damage exceeding their tech cost. Drake threatens 4 damage with 2 tech (positive shock); Valkyrie threatens 4 damage with 4 tech (no shock).
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Definition of community term during unit comparison discussion.

insights.insight: 'Big red' refers to any strategy involving a 3+ red cost unit such as Amporilla or Lucina.
insights.author: wiwiweb
insights.context: Definition of strategic terminology for high-red-cost attack units.

insights.insight: 'Big clock' units have 4+ :Time: in their cost; 'big blue' units have 3 blue; 'big red' units have 3+ red. These categories describe units with heavy color requirements that serve as economic sinks or late-game threats.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Definition of unit cost categories used in set analysis and strategy discussion.

insights.insight: "Value Wincer" refers to purchasing Wincer for its defensive value (15 HP worth more than 5 drones over 2.5 turns) without firing it, relying on tempo advantage. "Value Wincer Click" (or "Firing Wincer") refers to actually threatening to fire Wincer by then, gaining both the defensive tempo and burst damage.
insights.author: masn6811, Deleted User
insights.context: Discussion of terminology for Wincer holding vs. firing strategies.

insights.insight: "Fastimus" refers to rushing Animus for economy acceleration, often in aggressive or absorb-denial focused sets, as opposed to "natural Animus" which is the default mid-game buy.
insights.author: namington, cogito3
insights.context: Terminology clarification during strategy discussion.

insights.insight: Against Pixie-heavy attacks, buying Engineers preemptively for granularity is better than buying Walls reactively, as Walls are inefficient blockers against small distributed damage. Husks can be more efficient than Walls in specific pixie matchups.
insights.author: steel0229e
insights.context: Game analysis identifying defensive inefficiency when blocking pixies with walls.

insights.insight: Developer Elyot noted that StarCraft 2 has too much defender's advantage due to warp gate negating positional strategy, which was a problematic design that inspired Prismata's more balanced approach.
insights.author: elyot (Developer)
insights.context: Design philosophy discussion comparing Prismata to StarCraft 2.

insights.insight: Shalev's Rule (from Lunarch's original design specification) is a core design principle in Prismata: units that are individually weaker create more interesting decision-making through opportunity costs, and the game's adherence to this principle is a major design strength that distinguishes it from other card games.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Discussion of Lunarch's design philosophy and how Prismata maintains strategic integrity through Shalev's Rule.

insights.insight: Overall Player 2 winrate is ~52%, which is lower than many competitive games; set-specific advantages can exceed 58-65% (e.g., pure Redeemer mirrors) but true autowin sets are extremely rare.
insights.author: liadahlia, 307th, consensus
insights.context: Discussion of Player 1 vs Player 2 advantage across sets and how it affects strategy.

insights.insight: At 1600 MMR, most improvement comes from fixing mechanical errors (overteching, wasting energy in opening) rather than set reading. Watching high-level streams and replays becomes more valuable at 1700-1800+ when set reading is the limiting factor.
insights.author: 307th
insights.context: Skill development roadmap for intermediate players.

insights.insight: Apooche's inflation math articles are the most trustworthy publicly available analysis because they present graphs across multiple inflation values rather than single-point conclusions, avoiding the critical error of assuming a specific inflation scenario.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Community evaluation of mathematical analysis approaches.

insights.insight: When learning from bot games, replay a set where the bot beat you using strategy X, then play as the bot's side and try to execute that same strategy yourself; if you can win with it, you've validated it as playable and improved your fundamentals; if you can't, analyzing the differences reveals skill gaps.
insights.author: amalloy, mrguy888
insights.context: Pedagogical advice on effective practice methods for strategy games.

insights.insight: Base Set Only (BSO) has been extensively analyzed by top players who consistently conclude P2 is winning, though no exhaustive proof exists; the analysis is sufficiently comprehensive that major oversights are unlikely, but theoretical certainty remains unachieved.
insights.author: mrguy888, amalloy, mtanzer, masn6811
insights.context: Discussion of Base Set Only analysis quality and confidence levels.

insights.insight: A unit should only be considered truly overpowered if it has all three properties: high generality (buyable in many sets), high raw power (gives a large advantage to the player with access), and high set dictation (forces the rest of set strategy around it). Few units meet all three criteria simultaneously.
insights.author: junkmail
insights.context: Framework for evaluating unit balance and power level across the game.

insights.insight: Prismata skill can be acquired relatively quickly compared to chess, Go, or League—proficient players emerge within 1 year of regular play, whereas these games require years of study. However, top-level play remains mechanically complex and difficult to master.
insights.author: sano712, lyra1712
insights.context: Meta-discussion of Prismata learning curve and skill distribution.

insights.insight: Value math systems (like Yujiri's) are approximations useful for rough efficiency comparisons, but all single-value systems will be incorrect in some cases. A 33.3% interest rate model can serve as an alternative framework.
insights.author: anesotericman
insights.context: Discussion of limitations of unit value math and alternative approaches.

insights.insight: When crash_overlord plays, he figures out how to acquire his desired big unit on the earliest possible turn and becomes distressed if the opponent can acquire it a half-turn earlier, indicating precise tempo optimization is key to his strategy.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Observation of a top player's strategic approach.

insights.insight: Even with a much larger player base, top Prismata players would likely not be substantially better than current top players (apooche, msven, arkanishu, etc.), suggesting the metagame has reached a reasonably mature state.
insights.author: sano712
insights.context: Debate about whether player quantity limits game understanding.

insights.insight: Tarsiers are stronger attackers in base set than Inmolite, especially early game; Inmolite is only more efficient if it forces immediate defense or when offensive finesse matters significantly.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Comparison of attack unit efficiency in early turns.

insights.insight: According to high-level player Weiseguy, a good heuristic for buying Redeemer is to ask: do I have 8GGB and want more attack? If yes, Redeemer is likely correct.
insights.author: mqp (quoting Weiseguy)
insights.context: General heuristic for Redeemer timing.

insights.insight: Redeemer is viable in base set P2 response to P1 asymmetric access. P1 line DD DD DDB SDC wins on turn 5 against P2's TTD by using Redeemer effectively, and also beats P2's TTE with WGE line.
insights.author: mtanzer, silentslayers
insights.context: Testing Redeemer viability in base set mirror with asymmetric access.

insights.insight: Elyot Animus (DD/A opening) is named after Elyot Grant, one of the game's founders. It enables P1 to execute fast red rushes one turn faster than P2's standard double-Engineer opening, and is correct when the set features strong red rush units.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Explanation of historical opening strategy naming convention.

insights.insight: In sets where Matrix is available alongside Amporilla, the set is defined by these two units: Matrix is one of the best defensive units and shuts down rushes, while uncontested Amporilla in a high-econ game is game-ending.
insights.author: p0lari
insights.context: Explaining why a particular game ended quickly despite both players' early decisions.

insights.insight: Mahar Rectifier is approximately at par (baseline efficiency) for medium-sized attackers in Prismata. Some players use more or less efficient units as their par definition, but Mahar Rectifier is a common reference point.
insights.author: .bky_1556
insights.context: Establishing unit efficiency baseline standards.

insights.insight: Odin is a value bundle approximately equivalent to Cynestra + Infusion Grid that causes the opponent to overdefend by about 4 gold of value.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Valuation of Odin as a unit in terms of component value.

insights.insight: Iso Kronus is a weak unit from a pure power level perspective because Gauss Cannon already fulfills its role (absorb denial), making Iso often have very little influence on the set despite Gauss Cannon being technically inferior.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Comparison of Iso Kronus and Gauss Cannon's roles and power levels.

insights.insight: In a Breachproof vs Big Red matchup (set: Venge Cannon, Cluster Bolt, Fabricate, Zemora Voidbringer, Aegis, Plexo Cell, Doomed Wall, Lucina), buying Conduits early for Cluster Bolt without establishing sufficient permanent damage is strategically weak.
insights.author: p0lari
insights.context: Detailed replay analysis of opening turn mistakes in Breachproof setup.

insights.insight: Iceblade Palisman is the strongest unit in sets with it; the winning strategy is typically to set up BBA (Blastforge + 2x Blue sinks) and achieve two Iceblades per turn for overwhelming defense.
insights.author: cogito3
insights.context: Meta assessment of Iceblade's dominance in certain unit pools.

insights.insight: In Base + Centurion, P1 natural Animus beats P1 DDE, but both 2-Engineer and 3-Engineer lines have merit; whether P1 DDE also wins against P2 defenses requires separate testing.
insights.author: masn6811, mtanzer
insights.context: Testing results for Base+Centurion matchup analysis.

insights.insight: P1 wins Base + Centurion whether opening with a natural 3rd Engineer or natural Animus. Both strategies are viable at high level play.
insights.author: mtanzer
insights.context: Confirmation of Base + Centurion set analysis after testing.

insights.insight: Venge Cannon is a green sink and should be treated as a convenient attacker when you have 1GGG available and it does not disrupt your tech purchases. It is not efficient when you must buy multiple suboptimal units alongside it.
insights.author: anesotericman
insights.context: Expert characterization of Venge Cannon's role in deckbuilding.

insights.insight: Energy cost is often negligible for unit purchases in standard midgame style, but 3 energy at once (e.g., for Venge Cannon) has a real cost even in midgame that should not be completely ignored.
insights.author: .bky_1556
insights.context: Discussion of energy constraints in unit valuation.

insights.insight: Inflation-based math is most useful as a cross-check on intuition for identifying where you are 'way off', not as a replacement for context-aware decision-making. The 33-40% interest rate framework is helpful for caching generic comparisons (e.g., 'Lancetooth hold is generally neutral in Wall absorb'), then modifying based on specific game situations.
insights.author: anesotericman, silentslayers, xnor_gate
insights.context: Meta-discussion of how to actually use mathematical analysis in game decision-making.

insights.insight: Xeno is best when you specifically want to spend 5GBB on a unit with Xeno's particular characteristics; its value is situational rather than universal.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Expert perspective on Xeno's niche role.

insights.insight: In the Amporilla set analysis, both players getting Amporilla in a sharp tempo race is likely optimal play with best play, with the game revolving around stacking Tarsiers while defending; Gaussite Symbiote can disrupt opponent Amporilla econ but Deadeyes are too slow to prevent it.
insights.author: mqp
insights.context: Strategic assessment of complex set with Amporilla and multiple pressure options.

insights.insight: Electrovore (as a P1 red trinket) is very weak for P1; without a strong red trinket like Immortality or Perfection, P1 cannot make Elyot Animus work in mono-red.
insights.author: steel0229e, mqp
insights.context: Discussion of P1 Elyot Animus viability with weak red trinkets.

insights.insight: Perfection is a much more impactful red trinket than Venom Warden in most contexts; Perfection provides efficient red defense and enables better attacker choices, while Venom Warden only influences wall-only pressure scenarios.
insights.author: redrame
insights.context: Comparison of red trinket impact in different set contexts.

insights.insight: Chrono Filter is a superior alternative to Centrifuge in many situations because it provides good value in sets where it has strong payoffs, unlike Centrifuge which now requires very specific (mostly Vai-based) synergies to justify the investment.
insights.author: namington, masn6811
insights.context: Comparison of two tech-acceleration units after Centrifuge's nerf.

insights.insight: Mega Drone is the best red absorber in the game due to its ability to absorb in burst turns and lategame without committing to double blue tech.
insights.author: darkeisbein, swapgo
insights.context: Evaluation of Mega Drone's role compared to Wall and other red absorbers.

insights.insight: Endotherm Kit is not a low-econ unit; sets containing it often do not qualify as low-econ due to the unit's economic requirements.
insights.author: zera777
insights.context: Replay observation correcting misconception about Endotherm Kit's econ impact.

insights.insight: Endotherm Kit is not a bad unit in absolute terms—it is good in mid-to-high econ sets with breach-vulnerable defenses and limited vigilant counters. It is less situational than Apollo in some regards.
insights.author: msven
insights.context: Reassessment of Endotherm Kit power level relative to other legendary units.

insights.insight: Buying a Blastforge is usually the correct play when you have excess gold and don't want to invest in additional technology, rather than buying inefficient attackers like Bloodrager.
insights.author: shadourow
insights.context: Recommendation for high-economy gold sinks.

insights.insight: The Q key automatically collects gold from all drones without secondary costs and auto-selects a working defense and offense, useful for newer players unsure of their defenses but not necessarily optimal.
insights.author: kynarethnobaka
insights.context: Discovery and explanation of Q key quality-of-life feature.

insights.insight: When calculating defense against freeze, units you choose not to sacrifice are not blockers. You can exclude freeze options involving units you want to keep alive by simply not counting them as part of your defense pool—if they're frozen, they're no longer defending, but you also don't lose them.
insights.author: punf, mtanzer
insights.context: Spiritfryer asked for shortcuts in calculating defense vs. freeze when keeping certain units alive; punf and mtanzer clarified the conceptual approach.

insights.insight: Base set only is the simplest unit pool; adding even a single unit to the pool drastically changes timings and interactions. Random set complexity increases exponentially with more units, making base set theory only theoretically relevant.
insights.author: steel0229e
insights.context: Context on why obsessing over base set balance is not practical for real games.

insights.insight: Buying an attacker that gets completely absorbed by the opponent's defense is strictly worse than holding the resources and waiting to deploy the attacker when it will matter.
insights.author: awaclus
insights.context: Fundamental principle about attack timing and resource efficiency.

insights.insight: Holding a burst unit (like Arka or Omega) instead of clicking it provides defensive tempo advantage: the opponent must buy more soak to handle the held click turn, freeing up resources for the attacker to build more permanent attack. Holding is superior to clicking when wall absorb is available, as it avoids redundant defense spending.
insights.author: mtanzer, mrguy888
insights.context: Detailed explanation of defensive tempo mechanics with worked example.

insights.insight: In the Armory rewards system, re-flipping cards is optimal only if your current 5-card set matches one of the known value combinations (e.g., r2-r3-b5-b10-g2); a heuristic is to re-flip if you have 3 cards of one color that synergize well, otherwise do not re-flip.
insights.author: jamesa7171, consensus
insights.context: Armory meta-game strategy.

insights.insight: When evaluating unit value through math, inflation rate (R) significantly affects comparisons between units with different time horizons and resource costs; providing multiple inflation scenarios is more useful than single-point outputs.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Meta-discussion on proper methodology for mathematical analysis of Prismata units.

insights.insight: Mathematical unit analysis in Prismata is only as useful as the quality of its assumptions; lazy mathematical assumptions can produce outputs that are worse than pure game knowledge if they ignore critical factors like unit timing, resource interactions, and early-game dynamics.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Critique of mathematical methodology in Prismata analysis.

insights.insight: Inflation is a key economic concept: the rate at which resources earn returns. In Prismata, using resources inefficiently (e.g., holding a Drone for defense instead of buying Wall) incurs an inflation cost equivalent to the returns that resource would have generated.
insights.author: liadahlia, amalloy, consensus
insights.context: Foundational economic principle discussed in context of Drone defense.

insights.insight: Inflation (resource value over time) is asymmetrical between players: one player may face high inflation (needing defense/gold immediately) while the opponent faces low inflation (sitting breachproof). A player can have high inflation while their opponent has low inflation simultaneously.
insights.author: masn6811, Deleted User, liadahlia
insights.context: Extended discussion defining and applying the concept of inflation to Blood Pact viability and unit sequencing.

insights.insight: In high-inflation scenarios (e.g., red breach mirror), swords now are significantly more valuable than future swords, making immediate attack and Perfection clicks better than holding drones for later economic value.
insights.author: masn6811, Deleted User
insights.context: Analysis of economic sequencing in high-pressure red mirrors with reference to Zoo Expert mission.

insights.insight: Inflation is the core mechanic governing unit purchasing decisions; it measures the compounding return on investment (ROI) of units over time. Drone inflation is approximately 4/3 (1.3333) per turn assuming free energy.
insights.author: namington
insights.context: Foundational explanation of inflation mechanics and drone ROI for beginner players.

insights.insight: Calculating freeze threats effectively requires adding chill to attack, then subtracting 'fake chill' (non-granular chills where defenders have less health than the chill value), rather than just adding attack and chill.
insights.author: consensus (namington, totally_not_fbi, cogito3)
insights.context: Strategies for mentally calculating freeze threats during gameplay.

insights.insight: Area of Effect (AoE) in Prismata is defined as an effect that damages or affects all units in a region at the same cost regardless of how many units are hit, making it inherently difficult to balance—either cost-effective and broken, or ineffective and useless.
insights.author: cactus_wren, consensus
insights.context: Discussion of AoE terminology and balance in Prismata compared to card games like Hearthstone and Magic.

insights.insight: Golden armor is the buildtime invulnerability mechanic that applies to units purchased (golden) but not to units created by other units or effects.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Explanation of community terminology for game mechanic

insights.insight: The balance between buying attack early (forcing opponent defense) and maintaining economy advantage is the single most important balancing factor in Prismata.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Meta-principle discussion.

insights.insight: Prismata decision-making is fundamentally about broad situational assessment rather than universal rules; context such as what defensive resources are available, what your opponent is forced to buy, and what immediate threats exist determine unit value more than cost alone.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Meta-discussion about how to evaluate Steelsplitter vs Tarsier in different game states.

insights.insight: Prismata decision trees are extremely large even after eliminating strictly dominated moves, because floating gold, holding units, and various defensive options are rarely strictly dominated—they are context-dependent.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Discussion of AI search space complexity and whether brute-force methods could work for Prismata.

insights.insight: Inflation is the relative value of resources over time; as inflation increases (early to late game), the return on investment of attackers increases relative to drones because preventing damage becomes more valuable than generating income.
insights.author: .bky_1556
insights.context: Fundamental economic principle explaining resource allocation priority shifts.

insights.insight: Unit efficiency calculations should account for interest rate (time value of resources). A 33.33% interest rate per turn is a reasonable approximation for standard midgame, making recurring gold or attack worth 3x the nominal value.
insights.author: anesotericman
insights.context: Explanation of mathematical framework for unit valuation.

insights.insight: In Black Lab, the probability of flipping a color you don't have (like White with only Blue unlocked) is very low (~1 in 30), making it extremely expensive to convert shards into lab tickets for color-specific farming unless you specifically want Infusions.
insights.author: jamberine
insights.context: Discussion of Black Lab shard efficiency and color unlocking probabilities.

insights.insight: Supply levels for units follow a pattern: 1-2 attack units typically have 10 supply, 3+ attack units typically have 4 supply, with exceptions for units with special abilities or defensive properties.
insights.author: apooche
insights.context: General discussion of unit supply balance philosophy.

insights.insight: Resource floating is a core efficiency metric: floating excess resources means paying 'opportunity cost' by not converting them into units or defense; tracking this in replays helps identify poor decisions.
insights.author: medar, person11, apooche
insights.context: Discussion of how to analyze games by tracking econ, floats, and primary units.

insights.insight: Low-economy red mirrors are difficult to execute correctly in time pressure (45 seconds) because there are many optimal decisions per turn; 60-90 seconds is more comfortable for accurate play in these positions.
insights.author: mtanzer
insights.context: Personal reflection on time management in specific game types.

insights.insight: Set reading—determining which units are strong and how they interact—is the most important skill to develop after learning basic mechanics (absorb prioritization, breach avoidance), more important than perfect efficiency.
insights.author: wiwiweb
insights.context: Advice to new players on skill development priorities.

insights.insight: Supply (unit availability) functions as a resource in Prismata; currently, nothing benefits from low supply, and having higher unit supply always dominates having lower supply.
insights.author: namington, cogito3
insights.context: Discussion of whether unit supply depletion can have value under Smooth Dominant Resilience principle.

insights.insight: Approximations like '1 damage worth 2 gold' or '1 absorb worth 1 attack' are useful heuristics for comparing unit efficiency across different stat distributions, but these estimates become increasingly inaccurate for units with extreme ratios and should not be applied mechanically without considering the specific game state and defense mechanics.
insights.author: consensus (totally_not_fbi, ruinedshadows)
insights.context: Discussion of unit valuation heuristics and their limitations in practice.

insights.insight: In Prismata, you can easily prove that holding multiple copies of the same unit on early turns is strictly inferior—not just to a human, but computationally provable. This dramatically reduces the effective game tree below what raw state-space analysis suggests.
insights.author: velizar_, siepu
insights.context: Discussion of game-tree complexity and pruning in Prismata.

insights.insight: Steelsplitter's value proposition is highly situational: it is inefficient as a wall (6B for defense with no prompt) and as an attacker (worst in base set), but becomes relatively better in high-pressure situations where gold has low value and defense supply is constrained.
insights.author: steel0229e
insights.context: Analysis of when Steelsplitter's flexibility makes it pragmatically useful despite poor stat efficiency.

insights.insight: If your opponent's best play is to kill your Steelsplitter, holding it back to force the kill is not helpful—you might as well attack with it, as the outcome is the same either way.
insights.author: mqp
insights.context: Logical deduction about unit positioning and attack prioritization.

insights.insight: Promptness is a critical resource in Prismata because non-prompt units delay their defensive utility by one full turn, and the opportunity cost of that delay often outweighs moderate stat advantages.
insights.author: consensus (amalloy, xyotsuba, Deleted User)
insights.context: Multiple unit comparisons highlighting how non-promptness reduces unit efficiency.

insights.insight: Holding a Steelsplitter to buy another Steelsplitter is rarely correct; clicking the held Steelsplitter and buying a Wall instead generates more value, except when Wall supply is short.
insights.author: mqp
insights.context: Mechanical optimization for unit clicking priority.

insights.insight: Sniping and chill are distinct mechanics: sniping kills a specific unit outright before it acts, while chill merely delays or prevents a unit's ability without destroying it; this distinction is important for evaluating their strategic impact.
insights.author: steel0229e
insights.context: Clarification of mechanical terminology when discussing unit abilities and their classification.

insights.insight: When choosing between sniping opponent's Drones vs Tech buildings with targeted damage like Apollo, prioritize killing Drones and economic units (like Gaussite Symbiote) over Tech buildings, as Tech buildings can be rebuilt with remaining gold.
insights.author: consensus (amalloy, leary93, f300xen)
insights.context: Advice on snipe priorities for targeted damage units.

insights.insight: P1 and P2 starting drone counts are equal (27 drones each) as of May 6, 2017 patch; before that P1 had 26 and P2 had 27, creating a 1-drone deficit for P1.
insights.author: jamesa7171 (citing patch notes)
insights.context: Correction of assumption about P1/P2 starting resource imbalance.

insights.insight: Drone sizing (determining optimal Drone count based on target spending per turn) is calculated by summing the gold costs of all units you plan to buy each turn at your economic stride; if the total is too high relative to available defense, a faster or different strategy is required.
insights.author: .bky_1556
insights.context: Explanation of economic calculation methodology.

insights.insight: Resource production units (Blastforge, Animus, Conduit) are approximately balanced to produce around 1/3 of their gold cost in value per turn. This derived from Drone costing 3 gold and producing 1 gold per turn serves as the baseline for valuing other resources at roughly: Red = 1 gold, Green ≈ 1.33 gold, Blue ≈ 1.67 gold.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Lengthy technical discussion about resource value conversion and balance model assumptions.

insights.insight: When buying a Drone and Forcefield in the same sequence, the Drone does not reduce the Forcefield's cost to 3G on the same turn; the gold cost is always 4G for Forcefield (3G) plus 1G for Drone, totaling a 5G swing in resources the turn they are bought.
insights.author: mrguy888, spiritfryer
insights.context: Discussion clarifying a common misconception about unit purchasing mechanics and resource timing.

insights.insight: Extra drones beyond what you need should not buy additional drones themselves; if you have enough extra economy to buy another drone, your build is poorly planned.
insights.author: velizar_
insights.context: Extension of drone economy planning discussion to note the anti-pattern of extra drones.

insights.insight: In Prismata's discrete turn economy, a half-turn drone advantage is worth approximately 6.93 gold equivalent in value due to drone multiplication, not exactly 7 gold, because gold grown over half a turn compounds at the (4/3)^0.5 rate.
insights.author: spiritfryer
insights.context: Mathematical analysis of second player drone advantage in continuous vs discrete economies.

insights.insight: In high-inflation scenarios (intense attack pressure), spending gold on defense and mitigation is preferable to spending on economy (Drones), as the immediate value of defense exceeds the delayed value of economic growth.
insights.author: consensus (crash_overlord, leary93, miuna6933)
insights.context: Discussion of inflation concept and decision-making under pressure.

insights.insight: There is typically a very brief window (possibly zero turns) between when Drones are good to buy and when they should be sold; the transition happens rapidly.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Discussion of Drone economic transitions.

insights.insight: Master Bot has difficulty holding Drones late-game when Attackers are already built, often buying Drones when Attackers or other units would be stronger. In specific endgame positions, Drone purchases can still be optimal over Tarsier (e.g., Splitter + Husk > Tarsier + Husk), but Master Bot applies this heuristic incorrectly.
insights.author: masn6811, velizar_
insights.context: Analysis of Master Bot endgame errors in unit purchasing priorities.

insights.insight: Units that finish constructing during a player's turn are constructed before 'start of turn' effects are calculated; Amporilla gains attack from all Tarsiers built that turn, not just attacking ones.
insights.author: http404error
insights.context: Clarification of event ordering: Build phase occurs before start-of-turn effects.

insights.insight: Win conditions in Prismata differ from asymmetric card games; both players usually share the same win condition (having more damage than opponent can defend). Specific units become win conditions when they are so impactful that successfully activating them results in a very high win probability.
insights.author: xyotsuba, liadahlia, consensus
insights.context: Clarification of what 'win condition' means in Prismata's symmetric game structure.

insights.insight: Gauss Cannon and Iso Kronus are roughly equivalent in efficiency when all damage is relevant; choice between them depends on opponent's defense composition and how you'll spend remaining gold.
insights.author: mtanzer
insights.context: Discussion of unit efficiency in breach-proof strategies.

insights.insight: When evaluating defense against attacking units with multiple copies, it is never better to kill a Gauss Cannon than a Steelsplitter if the Gauss Cannon has 3+ health, since Steelsplitter is strictly more useful for defense.
insights.author: mqp
insights.context: Fundamental game mechanic about unit comparison in defense scenarios.

insights.insight: Gauss Charge and Pixie mechanic: Both disappear at end of turn and are not differentiated in damage/value equations despite Gauss Charge requiring Blue tech and Pixie requiring Green tech, because the resource type is context-dependent and both serve similar throughput roles.
insights.author: apooche
insights.context: Technical clarification on unit mechanic comparison during strategy discussion.

insights.insight: Thorium Dynamo is slightly more efficient than a regular Drone as long as you don't waste any energy with it, whereas Auric Impulse functions as a drone you sell the next turn, costing you one energy (you get 4 gold next turn instead of 4 gold + 1 energy).
insights.author: 307th, crash_overlord
insights.context: Comparison of economy unit efficiency and mechanics.

insights.insight: When attacking in mirror breachproof situations with Dynamos present, prioritize killing Dynamos over attacking units (Splitters, Shredders) because Dynamos generate future attackers and represent higher per-HP efficiency (approximately 6 gold equivalent per 8 HP vs 2 gold per 3 HP).
insights.author: steel0229e, extratricky, mrguy888
insights.context: Extensive discussion of optimal target prioritization in economy-heavy combat.

insights.insight: When you cannot kill an attacking unit fully and it can hold (save for abilities), you should usually target it instead of partially damaging Dynamos because holding transposes the damage but partial damage does not—unless there are sword-cost enablers (rare) or you need exact lethal.
insights.author: mrguy888, extratricky
insights.context: Technical discussion of optimal attack targeting in games with strong soak.

insights.insight: Thorium Dynamo is not playable when P1 can breach during the turn P2 has no tech and no income, or when 14-drone income is too slow for the set's tempo.
insights.author: .bky_1556
insights.context: Discussion of when Thorium is and is not a viable tech investment.

insights.insight: A rough approximation for attack valuation is that 1 attack ≈ 2 gold, which helps determine kill priorities when choosing between economy (Dynamos, Conduits) and attacking units (Splitters, Shredders).
insights.author: extratricky, p0lari
insights.context: Discussion of valuation heuristics for prioritizing targets.

insights.insight: In Thorium Dynamo sets, the value of attack relative to gold changes dynamically based on barrier value and breachproof status. Attack is worth significantly less when breachproof strategies enable efficient health scaling, roughly 1.5-2 gold per attack depending on context.
insights.author: liadahlia, mrguy888
insights.context: Discussion of valuation mechanics in economy-heavy breachproof games.

insights.insight: When defending with multiple Infusion Grids against frozen Husk soak, clicking 1 or 2 Grids are roughly equivalent (assuming optimal play from both sides), but clicking 1 is slightly preferable because opponent misplays are slightly more beneficial when there are fewer Grids.
insights.author: crash_overlord
insights.context: Teaching example about precise defensive calculations.

insights.insight: Strictly better unit comparisons are only valid when one unit dominates another in all constructed states; examples like Infusion Grid vs Wall or Shiver Yeti vs Rhino are not strictly better because build cost and situation matter.
insights.author: amalloy, vanitascabal6962, liadahlia
insights.context: Clarification of the term 'strictly better' in game balance discussion.

insights.insight: Drake's click ability generates a 2-damage threat that the opponent must respect, even if you choose not to use it; this is distinct from Drake's automatic attack.
insights.author: steel0229e
insights.context: Clarification on Drake's mechanics and threat model.

insights.insight: Purchasing a unit that does not pay for itself in value (e.g., a second Animus for Shadowfang) before confirming you will acquire enough copies of that unit to justify the tech investment is a fundamental economic mistake.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Explanation of how unit synergy and build commitment interact in economic planning.

insights.insight: Unit efficiency evaluation must factor in tech requirements: units with awkward or expensive tech are harder to deploy multiple times or quickly, even if single-unit efficiency is high.
insights.author: p0lari
insights.context: Explanation of how tech constraints affect practical unit value beyond raw damage-per-gold.

insights.insight: Savior is viable when the set has good defense and P1 cannot punish a 3rd Engineer opening, and when no significant Red attacker (Big Red) appears before turn 9.
insights.author: .bky_1556
insights.context: Explanation of conditions under which Savior becomes a playable unit.

insights.insight: With Thermite Core, if you have an odd amount of damage, you can click one Pixie to make damage even and activate all cores without wasting any damage value.
insights.author: .bky_1556
insights.context: Pro tip for maximizing Thermite Core efficiency.

insights.insight: 1RR click ability on Smorcus breaks no-click-cycles with Auride Core, preventing situations where opponent can farm resources without threat.
insights.author: .bky_1556
insights.context: Mechanical interaction of Smorcus click with Auride Core strategies.

insights.insight: Mobile Animus costs 1 more gold than half an Animus, making it strictly less efficient for single Iceblade per turn compared to full Animus (which produces 2 Iceblades and can freeze a unit).
insights.author: 307th
insights.context: Developer analysis of Mobile Animus efficiency vs. traditional Animus.

insights.insight: Absorb is defense gained every turn (passive), while soak is defense you have to pay for or buy each time you want it (e.g., Aegis, Plexo Cell, Polywall). Sets with strong soak but weak absorb tend to be slower because soak requires continual investment.
insights.author: masn6811, liadahlia
insights.context: Definition of key defensive mechanics and their game-balance implications.

insights.insight: The RNG seeding system in Prismata causes all Robo Santa units in a single game to generate identical output (same unit type), likely due to how the RNG is deterministically seeded per unit rather than allowing true independent randomness.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Technical observation about RNG implementation behavior in special game modes.

insights.insight: Grimbotch with 1 lifespan should almost never be used offensively when taking damage; defending (2 damage soak value) is vastly superior to attacking (1 damage output), unless breaching or exploiting specific positions.
insights.author: namington
insights.context: Correction of fundamental unit usage mistake in beginner replay analysis.

insights.insight: Wincer does not have to fire immediately when it arrives; if the opponent has no permanent attack, Wincer can pass and build economy, forcing the opponent to either build attack (losing drone production) or allow the Wincer to eventually attack.
insights.author: extratricky
insights.context: Discussion of Wincer timing and decision-making.

insights.insight: One damage from a click is worth approximately two gold (equivalent to an Engineer's absorption value), so attackers costing less than double their damage value in drones are economically superior to pure economy in the long term.
insights.author: xujhan
insights.context: Economic framework for comparing damage value to drone value.

insights.insight: Xaetron's charge mechanics: can fire twice in a row around turn 8-9 of holding it (assuming purchase turn is turn 1). Charges have Exhaust 1 property, making them similar in speed to Tarsier (Build Time 1).
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Clarification of Xaetron charge timing and mechanics.

insights.insight: High inflation game states favor immediate-value units (Rhino, Grimbotch) because the game ends sooner and tempo matters more than long-term economy; low inflation favors delayed-value units (Tarsier).
insights.author: swapgo, supergreyman, miuna6933
insights.context: Explanation of unit selection based on game inflation state.

insights.insight: Soak means sacrificing a unit on defense to absorb damage; frontline units like Shredder, Hannibull, and Polywall soak efficiently when opponent cannot attack for exact amounts.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Clarification of soak vs absorb terminology.

insights.insight: When choosing to click a unit with absorb (like Omega) versus holding it for defense, the timing advantage matters significantly: you buy defense first (bad tempo), then force opponent to buy defense half a turn later (less bad). Holding is usually superior because the opponent's forced purchase happens after their turn is complete, minimizing the impact.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Discussion of Omega click strategy and defense tempo economics.

insights.insight: When comparing defensive options, Urban and Wall transactions can be reordered without loss when sufficient defensive tempo exists; Urban becomes strictly better only if Wall supply is exhausted or if Urban's increased threat matters (wall supply arguments are rarely decisive).
insights.author: amalloy, jamberine
insights.context: Analysis of Urban vs. Wall buying decisions and supply constraints.

insights.insight: When opponent threatens to freeze an absorber with Vai Mauronax, if you hold absorbers in reserve to prevent fork scenarios, opponent can instead freeze Wall, forcing you to overcommit defense and potentially losing the reserved absorber.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Explanation of fork mechanics with absorber holding.

insights.insight: When calculating unit threat against absorbers, damage dealt counts toward threat ceiling; if a unit deals X damage, it has (total_threat - X) additional non-damage threat, preventing double-counting of defensive value.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Explanation of threat calculation when Centurion absorbs Vai's freeze attack.

insights.insight: When evaluating breach targets, prefer killing units that will cause immediate defensive problems (e.g., a Tarsier that forces you to buy soak next turn) over units that provide slow value (e.g., Vivid Drone providing 3 gold next turn), because in late-game breach situations, the timing of one turn is critical.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Discussion of optimal breach target selection and the relative value of eliminating attackers vs. economy.

insights.insight: When two players face off with only Tarsiers as units, the outcome is determined by the Fibonacci sequence: if one player has at least φ (golden ratio ≈ 1.618) times as many Tarsiers as the opponent, they win. The matchup follows Fibonacci numbers because of how Tarsier combat works with the doubling-down subtraction pattern.
insights.author: masn6811, amalloy, hyreon
insights.context: Mathematical analysis of pure Tarsier vs Tarsier combat outcomes.

insights.insight: "Shock" units are defined as units that immediately threaten more damage than their tech cost upon purchase, not units that deal high damage over time. Antima Comet does not qualify as a shock unit because it threatens 0 damage when first bought.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Discussion of unit classification and counterplay to high-damage units.

insights.insight: Barrier Value (defense cost per damage) varies significantly throughout the game based on available defense units. It ranges from ~2.3-2.4 for standard Wall+Forcefield defense to 2.66 (Forcefield-heavy), 3 (Rhino-heavy), or 4+ (holding drones). This affects attack unit valuation.
insights.author: anesotericman
insights.context: Explanation of how Barrier Value changes contextually in the game.

insights.insight: Absorb denial significantly skews attacker valuations upward compared to raw damage calculations. Burst attackers like Grenade Mech and Scorchilla appear weak on paper but force opponent to buy less efficient defensive units.
insights.author: jamberine, xnor_gate, mtanzer
insights.context: Explanation of why mathematical efficiency models underrate burst damage and its pressure value.

insights.insight: When holding Colossus for soak absorption versus rebuying Wall, the temporary soak gain on that turn (8 soak) differs from long-term efficiency; rebuying Wall the next turn costs 3 soak, so net long-run gain is only 5 soak despite the 8 soak temporary value.
insights.author: mtanzer
insights.context: Discussion of absorption mechanics and soak efficiency calculations.

insights.insight: Lifespan mechanics work similar to Magic: The Gathering upkeep—a unit with 1 lifespan remaining applies its start-of-turn effect, then dies at the beginning of its owner's next turn.
insights.author: shadourow, velizar_
insights.context: Clarification of how lifespan mechanics work when a unit has 1 remaining lifespan.

insights.insight: Economy growth rates (inflation factors) are abstract values derived from recursive unit economics and do not directly determine real-game value; the 2 free starting Drones/Engineers and early-game granularity matter significantly, and Vivid Drone's strength comes partly from being able to sacrifice the 6-7 physical starting Drones rather than requiring you to buy Drones to then buy Vivid Drones.
insights.author: silentslayers, awaclus
insights.context: Discussion distinguishing abstract infinite-game economy mathematics from real game scenarios with resource constraints.

insights.insight: Mahar Rectifier supply is high (10) because it functions as a primary attacker in situations where Animus is unavailable or undesirable, with 11GG/2 efficiency being acceptable for such scenarios.
insights.author: .bky_1556
insights.context: Explanation of high supply on situational attacker unit.

insights.insight: When comparing attack value to defense value (e.g., absorb), opponent's response timing matters: gaining absorb NOW has more tempo than losing attack NOW because opponent has already defended this turn.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Analysis of tempo advantage in buying absorb-focused defenses.

insights.insight: Urban Sentry is effective against small/fake threats (attacks that don't represent real breaches) because it forces the opponent to commit significant resources to overcome its absorb, wasting attack that would otherwise be ineffective anyway.
insights.author: liadahlia
insights.context: Brief observation on Urban Sentry's defensive role.

insights.insight: Resophore sets are defined by good absorb/soak combined with bad attack, where Resophore forces Forcefields to deal damage. Nearly every set benefits from Forcefields attacking, but only Resophore makes it mandatory.
insights.author: apooche, f300xen
insights.context: Definition and identification of Resophore set archetype.

insights.insight: Buying a tech building (like Ferritin) for resource spending should only be done if you expect to simultaneously spend all present resources and the new tech resources together; otherwise the purchase delays efficient play.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Discussion of tech sink efficiency.

insights.insight: Holding Arka provides inflation value because opponent must spend gold to rebuild defense while holder does not rebuild, creating a tempo swing that outweighs the direct 4 attack versus 4 absorb tradeoff.
insights.author: leary93
insights.context: Explanation of economic advantage from holding Arka.

insights.insight: The 3 gold per Conduit rule of thumb accounts for spending green on mixed-cost units and avoiding excessive banking; accuracy depends on set composition.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Explanation and validation of tech cost heuristic.

insights.insight: When facing permanent attack and absorb barriers, holding units to defend even with permanent attack can be correct if doing so allows a crucial purchase next turn, similar to how holding Valk can enable buying another Valk.
insights.author: consensus (1m2o, swapgo)
insights.context: Discussion of when to hold vs. attack with permanent attacker in tempo-constrained situations.

insights.insight: Whether to attack or hold with a Splitter defending multiple Tarsiers depends on the damage curves over several turns; in a simplified Tarsier vs Splitter scenario, attacking and sacrificing Splitter is roughly equal to holding within 2 turns, making attacking slightly better due to tempo.
insights.author: spiritfryer
insights.context: Tactical analysis of whether to click Splitter when defending multiple Tarsiers.

insights.insight: Holding a Scorchilla for one turn (not clicking it) costs approximately one attack value in efficiency, so holding Scorchilla is only worthwhile if you gain at least one attack worth of value from doing so.
insights.author: stacko, velizar_
insights.context: Discussion of Scorchilla holding strategy and the cost-benefit analysis of delaying its clicks.

insights.insight: The standard P1 opening in base set is DD|DD|DDA|TTD; earlier Animus leaves you on very low economy and makes it hard to get Blastforge and Walls without wasting Red.
insights.author: mtanzer
insights.context: Discussion of optimal base set P1 opening.

insights.insight: Standard P2 Animus opening is DD, DA followed by either attacker+drone or attacker×2+drone; DD, AC is also a honorable mention opening.
insights.author: .bky_1556
insights.context: Question about standard P2 Animus opening lines.

insights.insight: Early-game gold efficiency is critical; floating substantial gold (e.g., 4 gold on turn 2) is inefficient in an exponential growth economy because each Drone purchased returns 1 gold per turn for the rest of the game.
insights.author: p0lari
insights.context: Discussion of why P2's natural opener should maximize Drone and Conduit purchases to avoid floating resources.

insights.insight: The 'Standard Style' is the foundational opening theory for new players, emphasizing disciplined drone economy and safe early-game play. It's sufficient for intermediate play while still being grounded in high-level strategy.
insights.author: consensus, .andrees
insights.context: Recommendation of a beginner strategy guide.

insights.insight: DD/DDE opening should not be used as first player without good reason; requires either a big absorber (more than 3 HP Wall) or specific high-value units like Odin or Lucina in the set.
insights.author: shadourow
insights.context: Player asking about general P1 opening strategy.

insights.insight: DD/DD followed by DDA is the most standard P1 opening in general cases, developing attack economy on turn 3.
insights.author: shadourow
insights.context: Advice on general P1 opening strategy versus high-econ play.

insights.insight: DD/DTh opening is correct in approximately 90% of Thorium sets.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Specific opening advice for sets containing Thorium.

insights.insight: After opening DD DDE, Player 2 should not change their gameplan based on Player 1's turn 2 tech purchases (like Animus), and should continue with their planned economic build (DDD), as they opened that way specifically to beat rush strategies.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Discussion of commitment to opening strategy despite opponent's early tech.

insights.insight: When opening DD DDE and opponent responds with early Animus or Fastimus, P2 should not switch to DB; if third Engineer was the planned line, it would have lost to the rush anyway.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Advice against reactive tech changes in early game.

insights.insight: The standard high-economy opening in most sets is DDE DDD DDDB, executed when P1 goes DD DDE and P2 follows with DD (or equivalent).
insights.author: f300xen, consensus
insights.context: Discussion of standard high-economy opening lines.

insights.insight: DD/DC is the safest P1 opening in Tia sets because it doesn't over-econ and avoids losing to Tia pressure.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Master player analysis of optimal P1 opening strategy against Tia Threnody threat.

insights.insight: As P2, DD/DA and DD/DDC are standard turn 2 plays (often superior to DD/DD which floats resources); DD/DD wins less than 5% of sets and is generally poor for P2.
insights.author: pikachumemes, awaclus
insights.context: Analysis of P2 turn 2 opening theory and resource efficiency.

insights.insight: As P1, DD/DD is acceptable in non-high-econ games and differs from P2's context; floating resources on P1 is more recoverable than on P2.
insights.author: awaclus
insights.context: Clarification on P1 vs P2 opening differences.

insights.insight: A rough heuristic for choosing early openings: go DD/DDE (Double Drone or DDE start) if there exists a unit that absorbs better than Wall; otherwise, use a different opening.
insights.author: awaclus
insights.context: Player asking how to choose openers for random sets; awaclus provides basic decision rule.

insights.insight: In base set, P1's most efficient openings in a vacuum are DD DDE or DD DD DDA. DD DD is often preferred over DD DDE because it leaves the option for DDA or DDB on turn 3, whereas DD DDE locks into DDDB or DDDC.
insights.author: extratricky, liadahlia
insights.context: Debate about optimal P1 opening sequences in base set.

insights.insight: DD/DC (two Drones then Drone+Conduit) is an effective green-ramp line, especially when Fission Turret is available for defense; DD/DD/DCC is viable if multiple greens are desirable (e.g., Tantalum Ray, Plasmafier).
insights.author: mqp
insights.context: Player 1 asking for tips on opening strategies to compete with natural Conduit.

insights.insight: Standard Zemora Voidbringer opening sequences are dd dc ddc (zemora) for P1 and dd ddc ddc (zemora) for P2, prioritizing fast Zemora acquisition in passive sets.
insights.author: f300xen
insights.context: Discussion of optimal opening builds when Zemora is in the set.

insights.insight: P1 should not go DD/DD/DDEC (double conduit before third turn); DD/DDE/DDD/DDDC is preferred if committing to high economy, since it gives opponent fewer opportunities to punish over-commitment.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Debate about specific high-economy opening orders.

insights.insight: In sets with big red support units (Mobile Animus, Flame Animus, Chrono Filter), you may open DD/DD and keep options open for green development on turn 3, sacrificing one green most of the time to maintain flexibility.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Situational opening flexibility in specific set compositions.

insights.insight: In sets with aggressive units like both Grimbotch and Bloodrager, you should cut drones hard, sometimes as early as DD/DD/Animus with super-aggressive follow-up.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Aggressive opening advice for high-threat sets.

insights.insight: You should generally not build DD/DD/11 (drone rush into Conduit on turn 3); DD/D1 is preferable unless facing a very specific aggressive red rush threat.
insights.author: namington
insights.context: Whether to buy early Conduit or delay economy.

insights.insight: Against P1's DD DDE opening, P2 typically responds with DD DDE or DD DA depending on set economics; mirroring the DDE allows reaching turn 3 with 13 gold instead of 10, enabling better turn 3 tech purchases like Conduit or Animus.
insights.author: miuna6933, cactus_wren
insights.context: Opening theory for non-Savior sets with standard early game.

insights.insight: DD/DD opening as P2 is viable in specific sets (e.g., with Hellhounds or to stay flexible against rock-paper-scissors matchups like green vs. other strategies), contrary to general avoid-it advice, allowing P2 to commit later to the optimal strategy.
insights.author: amalloy, cogito3
insights.context: Debate on P2 opening variations and when delaying blue/red commitment is strategically sound.

insights.insight: The opening DD D1 DD is better than DD DD 11 for early economy setup.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Opening theory discussion

insights.insight: DD DDE DDC (3 drones, 2 drones + engineer, 2 drones + engineer) is a high-econ opening that is generally incoherent unless responding to specific opponent plays; DDB is often better. However, if opponent completely misreads the set or goes very aggressive (DD CA), this opener can still win.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Debate about optimal early-game openers and when high econ is justified.

insights.insight: DD DC DDC (P1 opening with early Conduit) comes out faster than DD DDC DDC for Zemora lines, giving P1 an economic advantage in Zemora mirrors.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Technical observation about P1 vs P2 Zemora opening speed.

insights.insight: In a set with base units and Smorcus, P1 opening with DD, DD, BA followed by Wall+Smorcus is inferior to alternatives like DD, DD, DDB or fast defensive tech. It lacks sufficient drone count given Smorcus's pressure.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Opening theory discussion in a set with early threats.

insights.insight: Double Drone/Engineer (DDE) openings are overly slow and get punished by correct P2 play; specifically, a sequence like dd/dd/dde/da/ddd/sym + drone/dddb/phage + tarsier/wall gives P2 a sizeable advantage, as the first Phage comes online on the last turn, allowing P2 to secure a Blastforge without wasting Red.
insights.author: pikachumemes
insights.context: Analysis of early base-set openers and their relative strength, arguing DDE is not a sound strategy.

insights.insight: In Vivid Drone sets with high economy, the 1EE 1DD opening is superior to DD DDEE for P2 because it generates 1 extra gold on turn 3 (12 gold vs 11), enabling access to stronger turn 3 plays like DDDD or 1DDC that the DD DDEE line cannot afford, despite DD DDEE being less committal on turn 1.
insights.author: 307th, mtanzer, p0lari, mtanzer
insights.context: Discussion of P2 opening lines in high-econ sets with Vivid Drone present.

insights.insight: P2 high-econ Vivid Drone opening: 1EE on turn 1, 1DD on turn 2, DDDD on turn 3 gives 12 gold turn 3 instead of 11 with DD DDEE, and enables flexible purchases (DDDD or DD1C). This line sacrifices option value for marginally higher efficiency if high econ is clearly optimal.
insights.author: p0lari, mtanzer
insights.context: Discussion of optimal early-game economy lines with Vivid Drone, comparing high-econ vs. flexible openings.

insights.insight: Player 1's high-economy Vivid opening (1EE, 11, DDDD) is roughly 1 gold more efficient than standard lines but can be punished by early Animus/Fastimus plays; Player 2's equivalent high-econ line is (1EE, 1DD, DDDD).
insights.author: mtanzer
insights.context: Analysis of high-economy openings in Vivid sets.

insights.insight: In heavy green sets with Vivid Drone, P1 can execute DD DDE D1C opening as a viable curve option.
insights.author: mrguy888
