insights.author: velizar_
insights.context: Discussion of Scorchilla holding mechanics and situations where it gains value.

insights.insight: Cynestra should almost never be purchased when Apollo is still viable as an opponent option, as Apollo will snipe Cynestra for value. Only viable if opponent is forced to commit away from Apollo or if you can stall Apollo one turn while pivoting Cynestra the next.
insights.author: namington
insights.context: Strategic analysis of Cynestra viability in Apollo-containing sets.

insights.insight: Cynestra is expensive (requires 12GGR to purchase in a single turn) and not all sets allow efficient spending of this cost; however, when you cannot out-greed a Cynestra player, you must either go under (rush) or mirror their Cynestras.
insights.author: cactus_wren, mrguy888, amalloy
insights.context: Strategic options when facing Cynestra-focused strategies.

insights.insight: In sets with multiple Cynestra supply, buying 3-4 Cynestras is difficult due to green color requirements for Cryo Ray support; typically only 1-2 Cynestras can be built with full Cryo support. Additional Cynestras become viable when Xaetron slows the game, allowing tech transition.
insights.author: xyotsuba, mrguy888
insights.context: Economic analysis of Cynestra scaling and color constraints.

insights.insight: When considering Cynestra in a set with Apollo, the key question is whether red-green is independently viable (not just Cynestra-specific). If the opponent goes triple blue (BBB), skip Cynestra; if they don't, build it.
insights.author: xujhan
insights.context: Meta-strategic question about unit choice in multi-color contexts.

insights.insight: In sets with Cynestra, low-economy Bombarder (Scorchilla) rush may be superior because Cynestra requires high economy for adequate defense, whereas Bombarder threatens quickly on low resources.
insights.author: velizar_
insights.context: Economy-dependent strategy choice between two attackers in same set.

insights.insight: Doomed Drone supply is limited and valuable. With 2 Energy available, always buy Doomed Drone. With 4 Energy, buy 2 Doomed Drones. With 3 Energy, prefer regular Drone in most inflation levels due to supply scarcity, unless in high inflation.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Optimization of Doomed Drone purchasing decisions based on energy availability and game state.

insights.insight: When deciding between absorbing on Doomed Mech vs Infusion Grid, holding Doomed Mech provides 5 tempo of value; the optimal choice depends on what other attackers you can purchase with the extra tempo and whether you are resource-constrained on wall supply.
insights.author: apooche, xyotsuba
insights.context: Discussion of absorption target evaluation in endgame scenarios.

insights.insight: Doomed Mech can justify a 3rd Engineer purchase, as it requires significant blue production to be efficient.
insights.author: jamberine
insights.context: Discussion of Engineer count requirements in sets with Doomed Mech.

insights.insight: When deciding between aggressive and econ strategies, evaluate what the set's key absorbers are; big slow absorbers like Doomed Mech and Omega Splitter support econ by forcing high spending on soak.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Absorber role in determining whether early pressure or econ is preferable.

insights.insight: Holding Doomed Mech while buying Feral Hound allows Feral's +2 health attack to defend via a 2-health gambit against Doomed Mech holds, providing flexible offense/defense options in the same turn.
insights.author: spiritfryer
insights.context: Analysis of a base+5 set game featuring Doomed Mech and Feral Hound interactions.

insights.insight: Immolite vs Tarsier: Tarsier is superior in long games, but Immolite's faster hit is better in endgame or when immediate attack is critical (e.g., games ending in 3 turns or fewer). Immolite also better when forced to spend all available red (6 gold) or when opponent must make difficult defensive decisions.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Comparison of attack ramp speeds and situational viability of Immolite vs Tarsier.

insights.insight: Holding attack (e.g., Immolites or Iso Kronus) to synchronize with Scorchillas does not increase pressure unless denying absorb; opponent rebuilds attack instead of defense during held turns.
insights.author: jonshea
insights.context: Discussion of why holding damage for synchronization is suboptimal.

insights.insight: Chronofilter is highly situational: it is strong in specific sets like those with Wincer, but is otherwise unplayable or merely another tech building in most sets.
insights.author: namington, consensus
insights.context: Discussion of when Chronofilter is worth purchasing.

insights.insight: Frontline units (like Hannibull) are good when you can soak with them while still maintaining full absorb value, but are often exploited if bought when the opponent already has sufficient attack to kill them immediately.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Evaluation of when to purchase high-health defensive units.

insights.insight: Spending significant health/tempo to deal 1 damage with frontline units (e.g., 7 HP of Hannibull for 1 damage) is inefficient; attack only if killing valuable targets or severely overdefended.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Discussion of frontline unit attack efficiency.

insights.insight: When going Hannibull, it's sometimes worth allowing your opponent to kill 2 Engineers and a Drone to get one click, because once you have Hannibull absorbing, those Engineers don't matter for defense anyway. This makes the Hannibull strategy easier to execute by keeping your opponent away from 7 attack.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Explaining sacrifice efficiency in Hannibull rush strategies.

insights.insight: Hannibull in sets with Chrono+Galvani+Wincer can function like a defensive wall-breaker and econ accelerator (similar to Thunderhead value), not just as a pure attacker; this dual role makes it harder to counter than raw attack.
insights.author: leary93
insights.context: Discussion of Hannibull's versatility in mixed attack/defense roles.

insights.insight: Mirroring Hannibull in Chrono+Galvani+Hannibull sets often results in one player winning decisively because one player ends up frontlining 2 attack from the other; attack-based counters (Pixie, Immolite) are more reliable than pure soak.
insights.author: extratricky
insights.context: Analysis of Hannibull mirror matchups and alternative counter strategies.

insights.insight: Hannibull is too efficient to get early in sets where reaching 7 absorb is easy (e.g., via Fibroid + Gauss Charge), because it allows the opponent to build cost-effective defense; delaying Hannibull is often correct.
insights.author: cogito3
insights.context: Analysis of Hannibull timing in sets with accessible absorb.

insights.insight: Wild Drone is worth 1 gold less than double Drone but enables turn 2 Animus on player 2, which represents a massive tempo advantage even when opponent can snipe the wilds.
insights.author: mwortel, supergreyman
insights.context: Discussion of Wild Drone value in enabling early Animus.

insights.insight: Exotic drone variants (Wild Drone, Thorium Dynamo, Vivid Drone) do not have preset opening lines; their optimal play is set-specific and requires on-the-fly calculation of gold generation, similar to standard economy openings. Mastery comes from playing the same opening repeatedly until gold/tech math becomes intuitive.
insights.author: wilson0862
insights.context: gex__ asked about optimized openings for exotic drones; wilson0862 explained experience-based learning.

insights.insight: In sets with Wild Drone available, buying 2-3 Wild Drones is typically efficient, but more than that becomes vulnerable to opponent pressure; strong players sometimes deliberately avoid overbuying Wild Drones to make their opponent's early attack less effective.
insights.author: strshplyr5046, mqp
insights.context: Discussion of Wild Drone purchasing strategy and economy efficiency.

insights.insight: Snipe targets should not be prioritized in low inflation if it doesn't deny opponent absorb; instead maintain pressure on defenses until opponent invests heavily, then snipe to make defense values bad.
insights.author: amalloy, Deleted User
insights.context: Strategic target prioritization in games with snipe mechanics.

insights.insight: Sniping opponent's overtech units (forcing them to carry too much tech) adds value beyond the drone killed; coordinating snipes with high-value targets can be worth pursuing.
insights.author: hologoddessofgrainandapples
insights.context: Advanced snipe targeting considering opponent's tech load.

insights.insight: When holding absorbers like Omega Splitter instead of attacking, you gain tempo advantage because your opponent must preemptively buy defense for the Omega's attack before you would be forced to buy defense to handle their damage. This creates a 'half-turn efficiency' advantage in your favor.
insights.author: f300xen, namington
insights.context: Explanation of why holding absorbers is often superior to attacking.

insights.insight: In sets with good absorbers like Omega Splitter (with blue sink), buying extra economy is often correct. However, holding clickable absorbers instead of clicking them provides more value than clicking them; comparing Omega Splitter held versus clicked shows holding generates more total defense benefit via better defensive unit purchases.
insights.author: jamberine
insights.context: Discussion of absorber mechanics and economy tradeoffs in defensive positioning.

insights.insight: Expensive units (12+ gold, e.g., Omega Splitter, Lucina) require deliberate economic planning to purchase; the player must reach a spending threshold before the expensive unit becomes available, then use big absorbers or previously-bought units as soak to defend while still affording the expensive unit.
insights.author: awaclus, p0lari
insights.context: Discussion of how to afford expensive units in limited-gold scenarios.

insights.insight: Sticking to a pre-planned opener (like Omega into Apollo against Rager/Brooder) is often superior to improvising mid-game, even if the improvisation seems clever at the time.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Player analysis of a game where deviation from planned strategy cost them the win.

insights.insight: In Omega Splitter-heavy sets, over-committing to Omega (especially the 3rd or 4th copy) without sufficient soak leads to rapid drone losses; Forcefields alone are insufficient defense if the opponent has Green units and can threaten multiple attacks.
insights.author: tmtyioh, spiritfryer
insights.context: Game analysis where P2 over-built Omega Splitters and lost drone supply despite seeming economic advantage.

insights.insight: With 1-Stamina Bombarder, holding it (not clicking) is usually correct because clicking spends threat equal to damage dealt; absorbing for 3 maintains threat without losing efficiency.
insights.author: cogito3
insights.context: Discussion of Bombarder stamina economics and threat value.

insights.insight: 1-Stamina Bombarder exceptions: click it to kill valuable non-defense units (Rhino, Drone) or to set up soaking next turn; 2-Stamina Bombarder should almost always be clicked.
insights.author: cogito3
insights.context: Discussion of Bombarder stamina strategy exceptions.

insights.insight: In Prismata, holding a Bombarder at 1 stamina instead of clicking it maintains threat (opponent must defend the same units next turn) while gaining 4 defensive tempo and 1 absorb to buy additional Bombarders, improving overall economy.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Analysis of Bombarder decision-making: when to hold vs. click attacks.

insights.insight: Bombarder at 9BBR is situationally valuable primarily when it can absorb damage as part of a Wall+Bombarder combination; otherwise its value is unclear.
insights.author: redrame
insights.context: Discussion of Bombarder viability in specific unit combinations.

insights.insight: Bombarder is strong enough that fast Bombarder builds are viable, with lines like 9bbr (9 gold, 2 blue, 2 red) potentially being higher economy than standard approaches.
insights.author: jamberine
insights.context: Discussion of Bombarder purchasing strategies.

insights.insight: When defending against early single-digit damage (e.g., 1 attack from a single Phage), buying a Steelsplitter and using it to block while building economy is often superior to buying a Wall immediately, unless there is already a strong reason to commit to blue tech.
insights.author: xyotsuba, amalloy
insights.context: Debate over optimal opening defense and Steelsplitter timing; xyotsuba critiqued earlier advice recommending multiple Steelsplitters for early defense.

insights.insight: Building lines with inefficient investments in attack units (like Steelsplitter and Gauss Cannon as primary attackers) fail to make efficient use of resources; focus on efficient attackers or clear economic advantage.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Critique of a proposed P1 opening line in base set.

insights.insight: Clicking a Bombardier to kill an opponent's Wall (thus denying them the ability to reabsorb it) is often correct play, equivalent to clicking a Pixie to kill an Engineer, unless you have a 0-stamina Bombardier available to soak damage instead.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Clarification of Bombardier click mechanics and when to absorb versus click based on opponent's absorb economy.

insights.insight: Clicking prompt-attack absorbers like Arka Sodara is generally bad because it forces you to buy additional defenders instead of attackers, which is inefficient; only click if it kills high-value targets or you have natural excess resources.
insights.author: amalloy, mrguy888
insights.context: Discussion of when to use prompt attack on absorbers, with reference to video analysis.

insights.insight: When you can establish an uncounterable Arka threat, accepting a Breach trade to get more total attack is often worse than continuing to build Arka threat, especially if opponent has limited follow-up attack to leverage the breach advantage.
insights.author: siepu
insights.context: Analysis of when to prioritize Arka over accepting breach gambits.

insights.insight: When defending with Sentry and Matrix, you must respect the threat penalty—opponent losing 2 HP to breach gains +4 damage (2x2 threat conversion), making it critical to not give up Sentry supply carelessly.
insights.author: shadourow
insights.context: Discussion of threat mechanics and how Sentry threats affect blocking math.

insights.insight: Urban Sentry soft-counters Feral Warden pop strategies and can be a key defensive unit in Feral Warden sets. Standard P1 Feral Warden lines include DD DD DDA TTC (Drone/Drone, Drone/Drone, Drone/Animus, Tech/Tech/Conduit or similar).
insights.author: liadahlia, p0lari
insights.context: Set analysis including Feral Warden and Tortle; discussion of defensive and offensive tech choices.

insights.insight: Urban Sentry is the best absorber unit and should be obtained as soon as you can achieve good value from it, typically around turn 4 in certain sets.
insights.author: jamberine
insights.context: Advice on prioritizing Urban Sentry acquisition.

insights.insight: When using Plasmafier, the goal is to convert all Drones into damage as quickly as possible; the longer the game extends, the harder Plasmafier is to win with, so holding Plasmafier for defense is very costly and third+ Plasmafiers are often overkill compared to buying Forcefields and Walls/Boreholes for defense.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Analysis of Plasmafier strategy emphasizing speed and tempo.

insights.insight: Shock units (Plasmafier, Drake, Grenade Mech, Tesla Coil) can deter early rushes by threatening burst damage, forcing opponent to slow down even if they do not deal consistent damage.
insights.author: swapgo
insights.context: Discussion of defensive units against early aggression.

insights.insight: In sets with aggressive Red units (e.g., Cluster Bolt, Nitro), buying extra Plasmafier attackers can paradoxically worsen your defensive position if they volley with your own units, wasting effective absorption.
insights.author: pikachumemes
insights.context: Analysis of why additional Plasmafiers did not improve tempo despite the player buying more attack.

insights.insight: Cauterizer is primarily a defensive soak unit, not an offensive attacker. It should only be purchased for offense if you can reliably fire it or are significantly over the absorb barrier with rebuilt Engineers.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Discussion of set reading and unit roles in replay analysis.

insights.insight: If not planning to attack with a Cauterizer, it should not be purchased as attacking red is worth significantly less than one gold in those contexts.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Discussion of when Cauterizer attack value matters economically.

insights.insight: Against Blood Pact Iceblade, you cannot simply kill Iceblades; you must breach and kill opponent drones. Killing Iceblades without breaching is a losing move. You need sufficient soak to enable breaching.
insights.author: shadourow
insights.context: Discussion of correct defensive strategy against Iceblade spam.

insights.insight: Blood Pact can be purchased instead of Wall for granularity in some contexts because it is not significantly inefficient, and it makes red harder to punish by opponents relying on red cost pressure.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Discussion of Blood Pact's role as alternative to Wall in defense and red sinking.

insights.insight: Against Tatsu Nullifier, the best defense is offense: rush the Tatsu player as fast as possible to stop them from buying multiple Tatsus, and Tesla Coil is effective at limiting Tatsu purchases to 1.
insights.author: jamberine, shadourow
insights.context: Strategy discussion for countering Tatsu-focused opponent.

insights.insight: When committing to expensive units like Tatsu, the player must actually buy them on the turn they become available; delaying Tatsu while stockpiling supporting units (Engineer, Drone) is a mistake because it forfeits the timing advantage and risks the opponent's counter-buildup.
insights.author: Arkanishu
insights.context: Post-game critique of delayed Tatsu purchase.

insights.insight: Arka (Tatsu Nullifier) should generally be held rather than clicked for attack unless: overdefended with tempo surplus, can kill vigilant blockers to justify the defense tempo loss, or have no other blue sink available. Holding Arka trades 4 absorb half-turn later for maintaining 7 defense tempo now.
insights.author: awaclus, leary93, crash_overlord
insights.context: Extended debate on Arka clicking vs holding and tempo trade-offs.

insights.insight: Clicking Arka is reasonable if: you have 10BB+ with nothing else to spend on besides walls, are overdefended by exactly 1, and can kill vigilant blockers to justify tempo loss. It should not be pursued at cost of buying additional defensive walls.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Specific conditions for reasonable Arka clicking.

insights.insight: Tatsu Rush (buying multiple Animus early for Tatsu Nullifier support) requires uncontested econ; opponent buying defensive units like Xaetron makes the rush vulnerable.
insights.author: siepu
insights.context: Analysis of Tatsu Rush strategy viability.

insights.insight: In RPS sets, Tatsu becomes the best strategy at high econ because you can afford tech while defending and buying multiple Tatsus; high econ breaks RPS.
insights.author: megasupp
insights.context: Explanation of how inflation and econ affect RPS set dynamics.

insights.insight: Thunderhead is bad when opponents can easily burst 11 damage (Grenade Mech, Cluster Bolt, Xaetron) or deny absorb effects (Thermite Core, Scorchilla, Lancetooth).
insights.author: willflame
insights.context: Analysis of Thunderhead weaknesses.

insights.insight: Thunderhead is a dominant pressure unit when present in a set because it provides both freeze utility and exceptional wall value; rushing Thunderhead before committing to expensive defensive builds is generally a strong default strategy if no other overpowered units are available.
insights.author: punf
insights.context: Set analysis where punf identifies Thunderhead as the dominant force and commits to a rush strategy.

insights.insight: Thunderhead is a strong defensive unit in sets with Vai Mauronax and Apollo because it beats aggressive (Apollo) and Vai rush strategies while providing defensive utility.
insights.author: namington
insights.context: Analysis of a specific complex set with multiple aggressive unit options.

insights.insight: Thunderhead is effective when opponent commits to early high-damage units like Lucent Hellion, as its 11 HP and permanent attack pressure is devastating against rush builds lacking efficient defense resources.
insights.author: willflame
insights.context: Discussion of Thunderhead as defensive rush counter.

insights.insight: Thunderhead timing matters significantly: buy Thunderhead only when opponent reaches 4-5 attack, balancing maximized absorb value against risk of early death, which generates tempo advantage.
insights.author: zera777
insights.context: Discussing optimal Thunderhead purchase timing.

insights.insight: Against Lucina, you must either rush Lucina yourself or apply enough early pressure to prevent opponent from buying it efficiently; a passive response allows opponent to stabilize with Lucina's soak and click ability.
insights.author: siepu
insights.context: Discussing why third Engineer line lost to Lucina.

insights.insight: When building economy in Vai/Matrix sets, aim for ~20 drones by default; adjust higher if strong red sinks exist to allow faster spending of tech resources.
insights.author: 307th
insights.context: Response to question about appropriate economy size in Vai/Matrix set.

insights.insight: Second absorber units are only valuable for defense when they can be used as effective soak; buying additional absorbers primarily to prevent opponent freeze effects (via Vai Mauronax) is generally inefficient.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Analysis of absorber redundancy and Vai freeze denial.

insights.insight: Buying Vai Mauronax to counter Centurion is often inferior to building your own Centurion, especially if your opponent has Phage; their Vai will be less effective without corresponding phage synergy.
insights.author: jamberine
insights.context: Discussion of reactive vs. proactive unit choices.

insights.insight: Plexo Cell is the best soak unit in the game. It provides efficient damage absorption comparable to or better than Rhino in many contexts, without requiring absorb mechanics.
insights.author: jamberine
insights.context: Comparison of soak unit efficiency.

insights.insight: When defending against multiple attackers, buying Polywall becomes efficient if you use its full 5 HP absorb and it saves you from buying any other defense next turn, but it is bad if opponent has granular attacks or you waste other tech colors.
insights.author: kristian1103, mqp
insights.context: Discussion of Polywall vs Wall defense tradeoffs.

insights.insight: In sets with Redeemer and strong attackers, getting Gauss Cannon + Fabricator early is often overrated compared to rushing Redeemer + Xeno, as the value engines do not pressure opponent fast enough before the combo player establishes inevitability.
insights.author: p0lari, mqp, steel0229e
insights.context: Replay analysis where player built Gauss + Fab into Redeemer + Xeno; experts noted greed was the mistake.

insights.insight: When playing Gauss Cannon versus Immolite, Gauss Cannons win specifically against P1 T1 Animus into Immolites. The Gauss player can pick off each wave of Immolites as they come off cooldown while also damaging drones, while the Conduit player uses Forcefield to block new Immolites and buys Engineers to stabilize economy.
insights.author: .bky_1556
insights.context: Discussing a specific matchup between two early-game strategies.

insights.insight: In breachproof mirror situations with Gauss Cannon, killing your opponent's Gauss Cannons removes their options if the damage can't secure a full kill anyway. This is more valuable than splitting damage across units.
insights.author: extratricky
insights.context: Tactical prioritization in mirror breachproof endgames.

insights.insight: Choosing between Gauss Cannon and Iso Kronus depends on the rate of inflation; when not overdefended, inflation is usually high enough that both work, but efficiency differences matter in close games.
insights.author: masn6811, jamberine
insights.context: Comparison of two ranged attacker units in mid-game.

insights.insight: In pure monocolor base-set play, the color matchups follow a rock-paper-scissors pattern: Green beats Red, Red beats Blue, Blue beats Green. This is because Gauss Cannons are expensive and Blue's absorb cancels them out; Steelsplitters are too expensive to breach Red's Rhino defense; and Red's Tarsiers can't effectively breach Green's Forcefields.
insights.author: hyreon, velizar_, jamberine
insights.context: Discussion of monocolor base-set forced play outcomes and matchup theory.

insights.insight: Breachproof with Gauss Cannons is only situational, played roughly 10% of the time in base set play. The low prevalence of its utility makes Gauss Cannons very situational units.
insights.author: velizar_
insights.context: Discussion of how rarely breachproof defense is needed.

insights.insight: When facing Gauss Cannons and Walls, sometimes holding a second Gauss Cannon to accelerate Gauss Fabricator is better than immediately ramming it into the Wall, because you can purchase the Cannon afterward when it deals real damage.
insights.author: .bky_1556
insights.context: Strategic advice on sequencing Gauss Cannon purchases relative to Fabricator.

insights.insight: Antima Comet in high-Engineer sets should be delayed until late game; an early Antima Comet will simply get soaked by Shredders, so setup through other attackers and defense is required first.
insights.author: p0lari
insights.context: Timing strategy for Antima Comet based on available soak in the set.

insights.insight: In Antima-focused sets, building Antima early with Conduit support and buying both Blastforge and Antima on the same turn is a strong opening because you can store green resources but blue/red resources must be immediately spent.
insights.author: willflame
insights.context: Explanation of Antima build timing and resource management.

insights.insight: If forced into having second Antima, prioritize buying Walls and Innervi Fields to tank opponent's attacks while building economy, allowing a delayed counter-Antima with larger econ advantage.
insights.author: willflame
insights.context: Defensive strategy when trailing on Antima purchase.

insights.insight: In a deterministic game with perfect information, the player on the losing side should ideally want to delay their loss as long as possible. Buying Antima first (the aggressive play) often loses when the opponent correctly reads they are winning and can defend with Blastforge/Conduit, then build their own Antima later.
insights.author: mrguy888, masn6811
insights.context: Theoretical analysis of Antima Comet equilibrium play and first-move advantage.

insights.insight: When deciding whether to buy Antima Comet, you must balance getting the first Antima (tempo) against building a sufficiently large one (power). The critical size threshold at which a delayed larger Antima beats an earlier smaller one is highly dependent on the rest of the unit set.
insights.author: ruinedshadows
insights.context: Strategic framework for evaluating Antima timing decisions.

insights.insight: When Freeze is in the set, blocking at 2 lifespan can be an effective way to deny value from Doomed Mech while avoiding excessive Freeze math.
insights.author: p0lari
insights.context: Tactical consideration for managing Freeze interactions with limited-lifespan units.

insights.insight: Iceblade itself is rarely purchased more than once or twice even in base+Iceblade sets, as other units and defenses provide better value.
insights.author: mtanzer
insights.context: Unit evaluation in a limited set composition.

insights.insight: Against Iceblade, buying two Asteri early while taking damage is often correct; if opponent doesn't defend the Asteri, you kill drones and win the exchange; if they do defend, 6 damage is enough to kill Iceblade.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Explanation of how green BP strategies beat Iceblade.

insights.insight: Iceblade builds are beatable when they don't all-in by buying multiple Iceblades at once. Single Iceblade per turn provides weak pressure and is easily handled by blue or green Blood Pact strategies.
insights.author: amalloy, silentslayers
insights.context: Evaluation of Iceblade's effectiveness as a pressure unit.

insights.insight: Frost Brooder becomes poor value if your opponent is getting Xenos because Xeno Guardian deals with freeze, and Frost Brooder does little against Resophore in the late game.
insights.author: elyot
insights.context: Matchup-based evaluation of Frost Brooder value.

insights.insight: Frost Brooder can be acceptable to deny 7 soak on a low-lifespan defense grid in specific sets, but it is generally not a primary defense option.
insights.author: elyot
insights.context: Situational use-case for Frost Brooder.

insights.insight: When building Hellhound-heavy sets with limited income, a single Animus with Blastforge is usually sufficient; a second Animus often cannot spend its tech profitably and should be replaced with additional Drones or Engineers.
insights.author: mtanzer, Deleted User
insights.context: Discussion of set evaluation where player had only 12 gold income and trouble spending resources with Hellhounds.

insights.insight: Corpus clicks are an aggressive tempo play (not a reliable defense); buying an extra Blastforge just to enable Corpus clicks is inefficient compared to using Walls or other defensive layers.
insights.author: masn6811, steel0229e
insights.context: Debate about Corpus's role in defense vs soak efficiency.

insights.insight: In Drake + Gauss Cannon sets, maintain a Drake-to-Blastforge ratio roughly equal to Blastforge count; overbuying Gauss Cannons or Splitters without enough Drakes leads to inefficient soak.
insights.author: mqp
insights.context: Player asking for set evaluation feedback; mqp identifies over-economying mistake.

insights.insight: When deciding whether to accept a gambit (attack offer while opponent is undefended) with 3-4 Drakes: if you can easily rebuy Blastforges while buying Shredders and Pixies with your blue resources, take the gambit. However, Pixie and Shredder/Infusion in the random set make not offering the gambit correct, as they make defending easier for the opponent.
insights.author: iminabearsuit
insights.context: Evaluating gambit decisions in specific board states.

insights.insight: Drake is a key unit that swings midgame momentum in certain sets; if the opponent gets Drake while the defender cannot pressure off it, the defender falls far behind in threat and loses the game advantage, even with solid mid-game units like Centurion.
insights.author: iminabearsuit
insights.context: Post-game analysis of a momentum swing caused by opponent's Drake.

insights.insight: When holding 3 Blastforges and clicking 2 existing Drakes yields poor value, buying a third Drake for tempo creates 2 extra damage threat that forces opponent defense, often justifying the purchase even in seemingly bad situations.
insights.author: steel0229e
insights.context: Tactical value of third Drake purchase for tempo advantage.

insights.insight: Drake clicking decision depends primarily on whether 2 damage now is worth more than a Blastforge (or 5 gold to replace it), with consideration for opponent's response commitment.
insights.author: xyotsuba, shadourow
insights.context: Discussion of Drake click valuation and opportunity cost.

insights.insight: Drake is often thought of as a 'fake threat' that rebates cost through extra enemy defense purchases; the true value is comparing 2 damage now against the defense overhead it forces.
insights.author: totally_not_fbi
insights.context: Conceptual framework for valuing Drake threats.

insights.insight: Drake provides value beyond being a fast attacker that generates Swords—it also techs you out of Blue by being a Green unit, which can be a significant strategic advantage by freeing up Blue-dependent purchases.
insights.author: cactus_wren
insights.context: New player discussing undervalued aspects of Drake.

insights.insight: Drake into double Tooth is a strong P1 opening in sets with Drake and Tooth, creating a difficult choice of Tooth-Splitter-Pop or Tooth-Tooth responses. The opening forces opponent to commit to building Walls while Drake denies absorb.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Discussion of Drake + Tooth synergy as dual-threat opening.

insights.insight: Drake Rush is weak into Wall because starter Engineers (2 base) cover the extra Drake threat; first Drake is not a high-value play in most cases.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Drake opening analysis.

insights.insight: In Drake sets, the Flame Animus opening and Drake rush are mutually exclusive strategies due to tech requirements. Flame Animus is easier to play as P2 but less reactive; a DD/DD/DBB opening is more reactive and harder to punish despite floating resources.
insights.author: jamberine, shadourow, masn6811
insights.context: Strategy discussion for P2 play in sets containing Drake.

insights.insight: The DD/DD/DBB opening telegraphs your next turn too clearly, making you vulnerable to punishment. In Drake sets, this line is weaker than alternatives that allow for more reactive play.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Critique of a standard opening in Drake-containing sets.

insights.insight: In fast sets with Drake, Lancetooth, and Smorcus, P2 should consider aggressive openings like float 4 into BBD into Drake, or Smorcus opening (DA into Smorcus+DD) rather than single Iceblade builds.
insights.author: silentslayers
insights.context: Discussion of aggressive P2 opening strategies in a fast set.

insights.insight: Against Thunderhead setup in Lucent Hellion builds, respond with Tarsiers rather than rushing Lucent Hellion; Lucent Hellion is strong but rushing it risks getting frozen, requiring safer early builds.
insights.author: Deleted User, mqp
insights.context: Live game advice where player rushed Lucent Hellion and got Thunderhead'd.

insights.insight: In an arms race set (or sets with units like Protoplasm and Sentinel), buying drones early into attack is more efficient long-term than buying attack into drones. The sequence of drone→attack beats attack→drone in overall economy buildup.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Discussing economy sequencing in arms race contexts.

insights.insight: Protoplasm should not be purchased purely for absorption value in sets with good soak; it's only worthwhile to buy Proto to cycle Xaetron when the tempo gain yields more permanent attack.
insights.author: jamberine, spiritfryer
insights.context: Analysis of when Protoplasm cycling is economically justified.

insights.insight: When good soak is available in a set, buying Protoplasm to cycle Xaetron is difficult to execute in practice despite being theoretically sound; the timing and turn efficiency issues make it awkward.
insights.author: shadourow
insights.context: Practical limitations on Protoplasm cycling strategies.

insights.insight: Floating resources to avoid over-committing to a tech color (e.g., floating 9 gold to avoid wasting blue) is generally inferior to accepting small resource waste in exchange for earlier tech acquisition and economic scaling.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Late-game economic decision analysis.

insights.insight: Player 1 advantage in Fabricator races: Player 1 can obtain Fabricator on turn 2 without floating resources; Player 2 achieves it half a turn later but gains approximately 2 extra Drones as compensation, which is acceptable value when facing non-Breach units.
insights.author: spiritfryer, itsotter, mqp
insights.context: Analysis of Gauss Cannon + Fabricator opening races between players.

insights.insight: Cluster Bolt rush is not an automatic win strategy and can be beaten by opponents who pressure effectively and deny the attacker's absorption, showing that set advantage is not absolute even in dramatic opening strategies.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Commentary on Cluster Bolt rush overperformance perception.

insights.insight: Cluster Bolt at its previous cost of 5/2 was stronger overall, particularly in non-mirror matchups, but weaker in scenarios where both players care equally about damage.
insights.author: mrguy888, amalloy
insights.context: Historical balance discussion comparing old and new Cluster Bolt costs.

insights.insight: Cluster Bolt provides critical green sink for sets lacking other green spending outlets, enabling higher unit counts in defense chains.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Discussion of Cluster Bolt's role in enabling unit-heavy defense strategies.

insights.insight: In Iceblade Golem games, the choice between killing the Borehole Patroller (the blocker) versus Tarsier+Engineer depends on whether the opponent can use Borehole as an absorber next turn; killing Borehole is favored if it would otherwise soak incoming damage, but both choices are often roughly equivalent in value.
insights.author: amalloy, f300xen, mqp consensus
insights.context: Technical discussion of targeting priority in Iceblade freeze scenarios.

insights.insight: Early Fugitive (turn 1) is very weak because it requires units to Core with and produces engineers too late; turn 2 Fugitive is situationally viable if the plan is clear.
insights.author: spiritfryer, itsotter
insights.context: Discussion of Fugitive timing in an early-game sequence.

insights.insight: Deadeye Operative requires a 4 Engineer base minimum for optimal play. The standard approach is to purchase Deadeyes while maintaining 2 Engineers, then continue buying additional Engineers and Drones after commitment, rather than stopping at 2 Engineers.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Discussion of Deadeye Operative economy and Engineer count requirements.

insights.insight: As Player 2 in Deadeye sets, the standard 3rd Engineer may be skipped in favor of Drone continuation because P2 risks running out of Drone supply while P1 sacrifices tempo with 3rd Engineer.
insights.author: Deleted User, p0lari
insights.context: P2 optimization in Deadeye sets when Drone supply is limited.

insights.insight: Deadeye Operative is a low-inflation unit (long-term payoff); it should be skipped in high-inflation (short game) scenarios and is primarily viable in third Engineer sets where income supports Deadeye spam.
insights.author: liadahlia
insights.context: Analysis of when Deadeye is appropriate versus rushing attackers.

insights.insight: Deadeye Operative can contest Wincer rush by coming out earlier; the P1 line DD DD DB1 2B allows Deadeyes to snipe drones while P2 commits to Chrono+Wincer, resulting in a close game if P1 plays sharply.
insights.author: leary93
insights.context: Analysis of P1 anti-Wincer lines using Deadeye.

insights.insight: In sets with temporary high-attack strategies (e.g., Deadeye bursts), spending attack on breakthrough is only efficient if you can sustain follow-up attack; otherwise, it is better to spend that attack to deny opponent absorb or build permanent damage.
insights.author: siepu
insights.context: Discussion of when to gambit for breach vs when to use attack for denying absorb.

insights.insight: In sets with Deadeye and Vivid, you should avoid buying Deadeyes because the opponent can convert Drones to Vivids, making Deadeye completely useless. Centurion is almost always worth buying. Shadowfang is the primary attacker but is held back by red sinking requirements once opponent applies pressure.
insights.author: megasupp
insights.context: Set analysis and unit interaction discussion.

insights.insight: When buying Deadeye Operative, inflation is low, so Flame Animus availability doesn't matter much for many turns; standard Deadeye opening (P2: DD DD DBB 1DDE) should be used.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Guidance on when Deadeye Operative is the correct pivot and how to sequence it.

insights.insight: Deadeye Operative can bridge tech transitions; for example, getting Deadeye into Xeno allows efficient progression between tech stages.
insights.author: megasupp
insights.context: General use of Deadeye Operative as transition unit.

insights.insight: Fission Turret as a first attacker can work in rush scenarios, but early Fission Turrets will be absorbed with low value because of their lifespan mechanic. Building them requires confidence in spending accumulated gold efficiently.
insights.author: 307th, darkeisbein
insights.context: Discussion of Fission Turret timing and viability as opening attackers.

insights.insight: Fission Turret into Thermite is high-pressure strategy, but it's not always better than Gauss Cannon. Fission Turret has the advantage of costing less and providing a Green refund, while Gauss Cannon costs more but may be strictly better in certain positions. Do not build excessive Fission Turrets if they will die before you extract value.
insights.author: cactus_wren, amalloy
insights.context: Comparative analysis of pressure attackers and their cost efficiency.

insights.insight: In Fission + Cluster Bolt sets, P1's standard opener is DD DC into 1DD, building blue early despite small economy because the early pressure makes blue absorption mandatory for both players.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Opening line discussion for a specific set combination.

insights.insight: In rush-heavy sets with units like Vore, buying Engineers is necessary to avoid being exploited, and Vore naturally synergizes with Engineers anyway.
insights.author: mtanzer
insights.context: Tactical feedback on a replay where the player neglected Engineers in a rush scenario.

insights.insight: Against a greedy opening or big-unit rush, efficient small red units like Vore and Perforator are the standard punishment. What counts as 'efficient small red' depends on set and opponent strategy.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: General principle for punishing greedy econ with efficient early red pressure.

insights.insight: Setting up Arkanide before the opponent can counter-Arkanide is generally bad in rushy sets. The correct approach is to be ready to defend against or counter Arkanide if the opponent plays it, rather than forcing your own Arkanide setup.
insights.author: kristian1103
insights.context: Discussion of Arkanide timing and setup in aggressive sets.

insights.insight: Mahar Rectifier is a large absorber; if planning to use Mahar as primary absorb, the opening should be DD DDE to establish economy before committing to Mahar, not rushing Mahar early.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Analysis of Mahar Rectifier in a specific +5 set.

insights.insight: Building 2 Mahars while skipping Blastforge is viable when Aegis is in the set with other good green units like Xaetron, allowing you to avoid early blue tech.
insights.author: megasupp
insights.context: Set-specific Mahar opening without blue tech.

insights.insight: Chieftain train requires predicting 3+ turns ahead to ensure 7+ absorb value; setup costs one Blastforge 3 turns in advance and usually requires a second Conduit, making it tricky but powerful.
insights.author: supergreyman, swapgo
insights.context: Analysis of Chieftain train setup and resource requirements.

insights.insight: Against a Gauss Fabricator defense, you don't need to breach early. Instead, put sufficient pressure that your opponent must buy defense instead of attackers, allowing you to develop attackers yourself while they're locked into defense.
insights.author: namington
insights.context: Strategy for overcoming Gauss Fabricator-heavy defense.

insights.insight: Oxide Mixer is best in sets where Pixie has significant value for non-attack reasons (e.g., absorb denial, granularity exploits) or where soak is very weak; it is generally overvalued as a standalone attacker.
insights.author: Deleted User, amalloy, totally_not_fbi
insights.context: Extended discussion about when Oxide Mixer is a good buy versus when it is suboptimal.

insights.insight: Photonic Embark's primary use is to play around threat (forcing opponent to defend earlier or in specific ways); using it for pure damage is only correct when inflation is very high or when hitting a specific damage threshold (e.g., with Hannibull).
insights.author: cogito3
insights.context: Explanation of Photonic Embark's tactical purpose beyond raw damage.

insights.insight: Feral Warden can serve as red sink that also defends, making it superior to Steelsplitter in sets where you want to attack more than defend, avoiding overcommitment to Blastforge.
insights.author: supergreyman
insights.context: Alternative blue sink and defense options to Steelsplitter in certain sets.

insights.insight: In high-economy sets, cutting a drone early (DD DB instead of DD DDE) to accelerate Flame Animus should only be done if the set is not too high-econ; in very high-econ sets, a third Engineer plus later Flame Animus is often superior.
insights.author: amalloy, cogito3
insights.context: Opening decision when Flame Animus exists in economically strong unit pools.

insights.insight: Timing of unit commitments (e.g., Scorch, Tia Threnody) is optimal at the boundary where going for the unit and not going for it have nearly equal win rates; committing too early or too late is suboptimal.
insights.author: Deleted User
insights.context: Theoretical discussion of optimal timing for unit transitions using intermediate value reasoning.

insights.insight: Sentinel is a strong unit but creates overdefense risk in sets where the only significant attacker is Hellhound (which also produces HP), and you must be careful not to continuously click Tanks and miss key economy builds.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Set analysis showing overdefense pitfall with Sentinel-heavy defense

insights.insight: Against Iceblades pressure, Arms Race is effective because it avoids giving Engineers value (Engineers would block Iceblades shots), whereas switching from Arms Race to Ampo after Iceblades are destroyed is typically too late because Arms Race is a very slow unit that must be bought early or not at all.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Explanation of Arms Race timing and strategy against Iceblades.

insights.insight: Tesla Coil is most effective when used to punish opponents who bought Animus too quickly, rather than as a standalone rush opener.
insights.author: deadhour
insights.context: Discussion of Tesla Coil win conditions.

insights.insight: Bloodpact is an exceptionally strong defensive unit and should be prioritized in many situations, especially when competing against defensive arms races where cheaper efficient defense is more important than attack output.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Post-game analysis of Bloodpact efficiency compared to alternatives.

insights.insight: When holding an optional-click unit like Thermite Core, you should follow through on clicking it if you built it intentionally. Leaving it unclicked suggests your initial plan was sound but you abandoned it mid-game, which is usually worse than committing to the original strategy.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Strategic advice on decision commitment and avoiding second-guessing.

insights.insight: Brooder is exceptionally good against Centurion because 2 Brooders can freeze Centurion every turn, denying its absorb value. However, buying Centurion is still worth it even in Brooder sets.
insights.author: megasupp, masn6811
insights.context: Unit interaction and counter-unit analysis in Centurion sets.

insights.insight: Pre-buying Xeno Executor to soak is moderately efficient; its main value is preserving Wall supply and spending Blue that would otherwise be wasted.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Discussion of Xeno Executor as a soaking and economic tool.

insights.insight: Perf (holding Lucina perfection) is weak defense against vigilant attackers like Wincer because it forces you to attack for only 4-6 into a Xeno Guardian, which is losing. Perf's strength is that it threatens but can also defend, which does not work against attackers that apply pressure.
insights.author: shadourow
insights.context: Discussion of matchups between defense and attack units.

insights.insight: High-quality soak units (like Aegis) come in large chunks requiring significant opponent attack (e.g., 7+ attack) before providing value, making it difficult to efficiently use multiple copies. This limits the economy-boosting effect of strong soak compared to strong absorbers.
insights.author: jamberine
insights.context: Analysis of why strong soak doesn't encourage high economy as much as strong absorbers do.

insights.insight: Proto is the best absorber in a set with Asteri and Iceblade; committing to second Animus to unlock Blood Pact + Proto is generally worse than second Asteri due to second Asteri's power, even though it would reduce Forcefield needs.
insights.author: spiritfryer, masn6811
insights.context: Strategic analysis of whether double Animus or double Asteri is better in a specific set.

insights.insight: Thermite requires low inflation to purchase consistently; natural openings include base set plus Thermite for P1 (natural Animus) and P2 (DD DD DBA).
insights.author: megasupp
insights.context: Establishing baseline Thermite openings in sets where Thermite is good.

insights.insight: If Thermite denies 2 absorb, it has paid for itself; don't feel bad switching to Walls as the game progresses.
insights.author: megasupp
insights.context: Value justification for Thermite investment.

insights.insight: Thermite should not be spammed forever like Lanceteeth; eventually it becomes uncomfortable to keep buying them, so transition to attackers. However, opening with Thermite is usually mandatory for efficiency.
insights.author: spiritfryer
insights.context: Guidelines on Thermite purchase limits and transition timing.

insights.insight: Buying Lanceteeth or Bloodrager without denying absorb is only worthwhile in sets with great prompt soak (like Aegis) where tempo has diminished value but permanent attack is also less valuable.
insights.author: pikachumemes.
insights.context: Situational analysis of prompt-attack units in high-soak sets.

insights.insight: Clicking Smorchling in vore/smorc sets reduces inflation and eliminates need for additional red consumption; this allows delaying blue purchases or buying blue purely for Wall and Pixie.
insights.author: steel0229e
insights.context: Discussion of Smorchling click strategy in vore/smorc sets.

insights.insight: Chrono Filter combos extremely well with tech-heavy units like Antima and Wincer, providing guaranteed blue and red units every other turn that synergize with late-game payoffs, though it becomes terrible in sets without complementary tech.
insights.author: msven, apooche
insights.context: Discussion of Chrono Filter's role as a combo unit and its set dependency.

insights.insight: Redeemer + Xeno Guardian is an extremely strong P2 combo that can be close to an autowin in many sets; P1 counterplay often requires technical units like Centrifuge to reach competing attackers without over-teching into green.
insights.author: steel0229e, p0lari
insights.context: Analysis of set containing Redeemer, Xeno Guardian, Centrifuge; multiple experts agreed on strength and P1 difficulty.

insights.insight: Redeemer should almost never be used to absorb (absorbing with it is 3 attack for 1 absorb, poor ratio); better to gambit for small breach than block with it.
insights.author: swapgo, supergreyman
insights.context: Discussion of optimal Redeemer usage and when not to block with it.

insights.insight: Redeemer is very effective against Wild Drone due to fast damage forcing the opponent to build defense, causing Wild Drones to become ineffective or die to Redeemer's charges.
insights.author: mrguy888, Deleted User
insights.context: Discussion of how Redeemer counters Wild Drone strategies.

insights.insight: Redeemer is strong against high-absorb defensive units because the defender cannot easily block all the charges; Redeemer pairs well with absorbent units like Plexo Cell that reduce charge damage.
insights.author: Deleted User, wilson0862
insights.context: Analysis of Redeemer's role in high-absorb environments.

insights.insight: Against Redeemer + Thunderhead, cutting drones for Conduit to rush the Redeemer first rarely works as a viable counter strategy.
insights.author: steel0229e
insights.context: Counter-strategy discussion for the Redeemer-Thunderhead combo.

insights.insight: Against Redeemer into Thunderhead, a high-econ strategy (e.g., DDC openings) is sometimes punished because it may lack the ability to defend at critical thresholds, but if the P1 econ opening is poor, P2 can greedily execute Redeemer first and auto-win.
insights.author: Deleted User
insights.context: Discussion of counter-play and timing windows against Redeemer-Thunderhead.

insights.insight: Redeemer + Antima combo is timing-sensitive: buying them simultaneously allows the Antima's engineers to arrive just in time to absorb Redeemer charges, making the combo relatively flat to inflation, but delaying risks not having enough engineers to make Antima viable.
insights.author: .bky_1556
insights.context: Discussion of whether simultaneous Redeemer+Antima purchase is worth the cost versus holding off on Redeemer.

insights.insight: Steelsplitter is one of the weakest units in the game and is primarily bought as a way to avoid wasting excess blue resources when better alternatives are unavailable, not for its defensive or offensive value.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Extended discussion comparing Steelsplitter to Wall and Tarsier, with agreement from multiple experienced players.

insights.insight: Steelsplitter requires a click to attack (forcing a choice between attacking and blocking), while Drake always attacks automatically because Drake cannot block, making Drake's click ability purely offensive.
insights.author: 307th
insights.context: Explanation of mechanical differences between Steelsplitter and Drake.

insights.insight: Steelsplitter is significantly weaker than Wall for early defense because it costs 1 more gold and is non-prompt, meaning opponents gain a full turn to develop before the Steelsplitter can block, making the total investment inefficient compared to buying Wall immediately.
insights.author: xyotsuba, amalloy, f300xen
insights.context: Detailed cost-benefit analysis of Steelsplitter versus Wall for early-game defense.

insights.insight: Steelsplitter is strictly worse than Tarsier because it does the same damage but costs more (6B vs 4R), and while Steelsplitter can defend, it cannot defend and attack simultaneously, making the extra cost inefficient compared to buying Walls for defense and Tarsiers for attack.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Extended debate on Steelsplitter valuation and when it is ever correct to buy over Tarsiers.

insights.insight: Steelsplitter is almost always better than Forcefield as an absorber because a 3 HP attacker blocking is superior to a 2 HP fragile wall. The only niche exception is with Resophore, where Forcefield can generate Tarsier vigilant units.
insights.author: velizar_, shadourow, silentslayers
insights.context: Debate over whether Forcefield or Steelsplitter is better for absorption.

insights.insight: Steelsplitter is better than Rhino as a defender when you have spare colored resources, because you pay 1 gold per turn to enable repeated attacks, gain +1 health absorption, and create 1 threat per turn when the unit is absorbed (threat is worth a fraction of a gold).
insights.author: shadourow
insights.context: Unit comparison discussing the efficiency of Steelsplitter versus Rhino in defense scenarios.

insights.insight: Apollo snipe is most effective for preventing specific high-impact unit purchases (like Antima Comet or Ark) rather than sniping cheap units like Conduit or Steelsplitter, which provide less strategic value than the cost invested in Kinetic Driver.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Discussion of high-value snipe targets and why sniping cheap units rarely justifies the cost.

insights.insight: Apollo is weak unless sniping high-value targets (Vivid Drone, Lucina) that it can prevent; sniping Tarsier or Wall provides minimal value due to Apollo's cost and buildtime.
insights.author: namington
insights.context: Discussion of Apollo effectiveness and target selection.

insights.insight: Apollo can be good when opponent's other attackers (e.g., Lucina) would be absorbed on first hit; committing to Lucina after opponent commits to Apollo creates favorable positioning.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Discussion of Lucina vs Apollo buildup sequencing.

insights.insight: Apollo is a significant investment that may not be worth the cost against opponents building primarily Doomed Mechs and Hannibulls, as those units are better countered with green resources (like more Zemora).
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Expert criticism of blue unit investment in specific matchup.

insights.insight: Apollo sniping Conduits from a Zemora Voidbringer economy is generally low-value because the opponent can simply pre-build extra Conduits and float green, making the snipe threat more potent than the actual execution.
insights.author: .bky_1556, apooche
insights.context: Analysis of Apollo's snipe targets and Zemora Voidbringer economy interaction.

insights.insight: In base+Apollo+Scorchilla, Tarsier is likely stronger than Scorchilla because: (1) Scorchilla is more vulnerable to Apollo than Tarsier, making red players reluctant to buy them versus Apollo-based opponents; (2) Scorchilla is not significantly better than Tarsier in a pure red mirror to justify detouring to green for a Conduit.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Analyzing hypothetical set composition of base + two specific units.

insights.insight: Apollo is slow, clunky, and expensive (BBB cost with BT2); it deals roughly ~3 Swords worth of damage per turn (6 gold equivalent), similar to Cynestra but without equivalent efficiency or flexible tech.
insights.author: liadahlia
insights.context: Valuation of Apollo's snipe damage compared to other premium attackers.

insights.insight: Against Apollo, sniping Perforators is inefficient (worse value than sniping Tarsiers), but sniping Animus can be valuable if it disrupts opponent's red consumption or Iceblade purchases, though other blue attackers typically provide better value.
insights.author: namington, shadourow
insights.context: Discussion of target prioritization for Apollo in mixed unit sets.

insights.insight: Apollo and Arms Race are weak units that should only be bought when there is an absorber in the set to enable slow games. Without absorb, these units lack sufficient value.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Analysis of when slow-game enabling units are viable.

insights.insight: Apollo's sniping of Vivid Drones is powerful long-term value; Infusion Grid provides additional soak on top of base Wall supply, buying time for Apollo to amass damage; Militia provides Blue sink for excess Energy.
insights.author: spiritfryer
insights.context: Challenge solution showing Apollo + Infusion Grid + Militia synergy against Vivid Drone economy.

insights.insight: Rhino spam (early Rhino purchases) prevents the opponent from freely droning and mirroring Tarsiers; it is an effective counter to early Tarsier builds in base set.
insights.author: steel0229e (tested vs 307th)
insights.context: Testing of P1 offensive strategies in base set against P2's Tarsier strategy.

insights.insight: Rhino has multiple legitimate use cases: as a gambit blocker against inefficient threat or when opponent has a valuable rearmost defender; as a first absorber in multicolor openings (bridge rhinos); and as a soak option when more efficient defense is unavailable. However, buying Rhino purely for soak when better alternatives exist is often a mistake.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Extended discussion of Rhino usage patterns and when buying Rhino is justified.

insights.insight: When your opponent has very valuable rearmost defenders (like units that absorb effectively), using Rhino as a gambit blocker can force them to not click absorb, saving you multiple points of defense value. Combined with Feral, this is even more powerful.
insights.author: .bky_1556
insights.context: Explaining Rhino's value in protecting valuable defenders from absorb.

insights.insight: Buying Rhino as a response to early attackers that can hold (like Steelsplitter or Perfector) is inefficient because the attacker just holds as the absorber and denies Rhino's attack value.
insights.author: extratricky
insights.context: Discussion of poor Rhino responses to holding attackers.

insights.insight: Rhino in Hellhound sets is valuable not just for the defense, but because the exact 1-damage increment is awkward for the opponent, and buying Rhino avoids forcing Wall which would deny Hellhound purchases.
insights.author: jamberine
insights.context: Detailed analysis of Rhino's utility in Hellhound-based defense.

insights.insight: When already over the absorb barrier, Rhinos are usually better than continuing to buy Tarsiers.
insights.author: siepu
insights.context: Unit efficiency comparison for post-barrier attack building.

insights.insight: Rhino absorbing prompt damage while attacking twice is a good defensive trade-off, especially against threats like Frostbite.
insights.author: pikachumemes., amalloy
insights.context: Discussion on actual value realized from Rhino in realistic game scenarios.

insights.insight: Vivid Drone has an energy cost of 2, making it more efficient than regular Drone; an Engineer purchased specifically to generate energy for Vivids effectively generates 1 gold per turn in net econ advantage after ~2 turns of Vivid production.
insights.author: 307th
insights.context: Discussion of when to buy Engineers to support Vivid Drone economy.

insights.insight: Vivid Drone is economically similar to Drone: it costs 1 less gold but 1 more energy, sacrifices a drone for net +1 gold per turn, making it cheaper upfront but more vulnerable to breaches.
insights.author: namington
insights.context: Explanation of how Vivid Drone's cost structure compares to baseline Drone.

insights.insight: Vivid Drone is most valuable when you have unspent Energy you wouldn't otherwise use, effectively converting excess Energy into extra Drones. It enables high-econ openers like 1EE 11 DDDD as Player 1, but such openings are vulnerable to rushing and should not be attempted in every Vivid Drone set.
insights.author: amalloy, miuna6933
insights.context: Discussion of Vivid Drone valuation and economy strategy.

insights.insight: Apollo hard counters Vivid Drone; without Apollo in the set, Vivid may be overpowered, and other units (Polywall, Doomed Wall, Infusion Grid, Energy Matrix, Centurion) make Apollo more buyable and balance the Vivid choice.
insights.author: mrguy888
insights.context: Unit balance discussion.

insights.insight: Vivid Drone + Redeemer opener must commit to either building multiple Redeemers (and skipping Vivid) or building Vivid for economy (and skipping extra Redeemers), as building both leaves insufficient resources for adequate defense.
insights.author: crash_overlord
insights.context: Analysis of resource allocation tension in Vivid/Redeemer sets.

insights.insight: Vivid Drone is not high-priority snipe target in Zemora Voidbringer sets; Vivid provides +3 Gold while Conduit provides ~+1 Attack and +1 Gold, roughly equivalent value, so sniping Vivid vs Conduit is not a meaningful decision.
insights.author: pikachumemes, silentslayers
insights.context: Discussion of sniping priorities when Zemora Voidbringer can target key economy units.

insights.insight: Kinetic Driver as first attacker is generally better than Redeemer as first attacker; if you get Kinetic first then Redeemer next turn, you gain 4 out of 6 turns of attack value from Redeemer.
insights.author: p0lari
insights.context: Analysis of opening sequencing in tricolor sets with Kinetic Driver and Redeemer.

insights.insight: Amporilla is one of the most difficult units to evaluate because when you have time to set it up properly, it outperforms almost everything else in the game, but it is clunky enough to make this setup difficult in most positions.
insights.author: p0lari
insights.context: Discussion of Amporilla's evaluation difficulty and conditional strength.

insights.insight: Amporilla is effectively irrelevant in base+Amporilla+Grimbotch as a three-unit set—the optimal strategy is to play Grimbotch+Tarsier regardless of whether Amporilla exists.
insights.author: mqp
insights.context: Analysis of unit viability in a specific set composition.

insights.insight: Amporilla and Lucina are primary examples of win conditions: large units whose successful activation often guarantees victory. Shadowfang is a win condition to a lesser extent. The emphasis is on achieving the conditions for these units to operate effectively (e.g., 4+ Tarsiers for Ampo) rather than just buying the unit.
insights.author: liadahlia, xyotsuba
insights.context: Examples of impactful units that serve as win conditions.

insights.insight: Big Red units (Amporilla, Vai Mauronax, Tatsu Nullifier, Shadowfang) require heavy Red tech commitment for 3-4 Red cost, leaving the player extremely vulnerable on defense, making them high-risk high-reward plays.
insights.author: 307th
insights.context: Blog post analysis of large Red unit costs and tradeoffs.

insights.insight: When facing Amporilla, it is usually not correct to destroy a Bloodrager over a Tarsier, because the Bloodrager generates less immediate damage output. However, if your future damage will decrease significantly, it may become correct to kill the Rager while you can deal the extra damage.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Tactical prioritization discussion in Amporilla sets.

insights.insight: Galvani + Antima Comet is a huge combo that should be pursued in almost every set where both are available, with equilibrium Antima Comet timings in Galvani sets being earlier than in other sets.
insights.author: elyot
insights.context: Expert assessment of a powerful unit interaction and its timing implications.

insights.insight: Engineer + Galvani Drone combination is roughly equal to a standard Drone in efficiency, but Galvani + Antima Comet is exceptionally strong ('disgusting'), and the winner is often decided by the first Antima in such sets.
insights.author: xujhan
insights.context: Specific synergy evaluation between alternate drones and powerful units.

insights.insight: Galvani Drone is a superior alternative to standard Drones when you never want to build another Drone again, being significantly cheaper while serving the same economic role.
insights.author: crash_overlord
insights.context: Explanation of Galvani Drone's niche as a late-game drone replacement.

insights.insight: Galvani Drone has minimal set-warping influence compared to units like Centurion; it mainly enables red rushes and provides a small boost to Antima Comet fastimus lines by generating free gold while fitting into the tech requirements.
insights.author: namington, masn6811, amalloy
insights.context: Analysis of how much different units influence the strategic direction of sets they appear in.

insights.insight: Resophore benefits from high Engineer count because once Resophore is 3-4 turns away, Drones become a better investment than Tarsiers; with extra Engineers available and free Energy, more Drones can be produced even sooner.
insights.author: elyot
insights.context: Expert explanation of Resophore's scaling dynamics and why Engineers amplify its strength.

insights.insight: Resophore is an effective follow-up to Comet because the defensive infrastructure built for Comet carries over well, allowing the Comet player to go for the best attacker in the set.
insights.author: mtanzer
insights.context: Sequential attacker selection in high-soak sets.

insights.insight: Resophore is weak when both good attackers and good soak are present, because soak counters the large absorber that Resophore builds. However, if soak is present without strong attackers, Resophore's long build time actually mitigates the downside of soak by the time it fires. Train units (lifespan attackers) are theoretically good against Resophore but hard to set up early enough.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Analysis of Resophore matchups against soak and train units, with discussion of when Resophore is favored.

insights.insight: Green soak (Infusion Grid) specifically helps Resophore more than other soak types, because the Resophore player's economy goes down naturally with a large unit, and using green on soak to save Forcefields is better than buying additional Forcefields or losing economy.
insights.author: kristian1103
insights.context: Specific analysis of how different soak types interact with Resophore.

insights.insight: Xeno Guardian should be primarily evaluated as an attacker, with the ability to use it as late-game soak being a secondary benefit; its main strength lies in dealing with threat-based units and freeze.
insights.author: medar, crash_overlord
insights.context: Correction of common misunderstanding about Xeno Guardian's primary role.

insights.insight: Xeno Guardian and Urban Sentry subsequent copies are less valuable than the first if threatened damage is low, similar to Wall comparison. Urban Sentry is particularly good because it absorbs for 2; multiple Sentries work best against chill/threat when opponent is forced to spread damage.
insights.author: awaclus, leary93
insights.context: Discussion of diminishing returns on buying multiple copies of threat-absorbing units.

insights.insight: Ebb Turbine allows late-game conversion of Drones into Attackers or Defense by providing a high-tech sink for Blue; it is most valuable after the 'halfway point' of the game when Drones become less cost-efficient than attack, especially with high-tech units like Vai, Lucina, or Colossus.
insights.author: namington, mqp, crash_overlord
insights.context: Players discussing the correct use case and timing for Ebb Turbine in set evaluation.

insights.insight: Ebb Turbine is a breach-proof drone in theory but rarely practical in blue sets because: (1) it is expensive (4 blue), (2) blue is usually better spent on absorbers like Forcefield, and (3) players rarely build 2+ Ebbs except in extremely slow defensive sets with no meaningful threats.
insights.author: steel0229e, mtanzer consensus
insights.context: Extended discussion of when and why Ebb Turbine is bought; consensus that 1–2 copies are maximum in typical games.

insights.insight: Ebb Turbine should never be clicked to buy a Drone in normal play because it wastes a unit slot and blue resource with no net effect; only theoretical edge cases like Golden Armor make this play relevant.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Clarification that Ebb Turbine clicking to generate Drones is not a valid strategy.

insights.insight: Perforator is primarily a utility/defensive soak unit, not a main attacker. Buying it as a primary offensive unit effectively costs 6R when accounting for the red production needed, making Tarsier spam vastly more efficient for attack.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Detailed analysis of Perforator's role and cost-efficiency compared to Tarsier.

insights.insight: Perforator's delayed build time (2 turns to first attack) effectively costs attackers ~2 damage per turn compared to Tarsier, equivalent to giving the opponent ~4G per turn, making Perforator-based offenses cost approximately 6R per effective attack when fully considered.
insights.author: amalloy
insights.context: Mathematical analysis of why Tarsier dominates as a red attacker despite Perforator's cheaper upfront cost.

insights.insight: Perforator can be used efficiently as an early attacker paired with economy like Animus; the ability to sell Red later allows flexibility to stop spending Red and pivot to defense or other priorities.
insights.author: shadourow
insights.context: Discussion of Perforator's versatility as both attacker and economic outlet.

insights.insight: Two Shiver Yetis against Defense Grid force the opponent to over-defend by 6 damage or lose either absorb or granularity, but this is only worthwhile if you can create an additional inconvenience (like a granularity exploit) the turn after buying them.
insights.author: .bky_1556
insights.context: Analysis of Shiver Yeti's effectiveness as a multi-copy unit against Defense Grid.

insights.insight: Shiver Yeti with Valkyrion functions like an infinite-stamina Rhino with +1 threat, making it an extremely valuable synergy for permanent attack pressure.
insights.author: p0lari, darkeisbein
insights.context: Analysis of Shiver Yeti utility with Valkyrion.

insights.insight: In Centurion + Xeno sets, 3 Shiver Yeti only denies 2 damage (not 3) since Xeno is not frozen; Shiver Yeti is weak in this configuration.
insights.author: supergreyman
insights.context: Discussion of Shiver Yeti vs Xenophage interaction.

insights.insight: Shiver Yeti with Valkyrion creates a permanent freeze effect (treated as Rhino-like pressure with additional freeze threat); the combination is strong enough that Valkyrion is generally worth acquiring for Yeti synergy.
insights.author: wilson0862, redrame, consensus
insights.context: Unit synergy evaluation.

insights.insight: Tatsu Nullifier is a strong counter to Doomed Wall because it can destroy the wall before it becomes an efficient defense, making it valuable in sets where both players are building tall green units.
insights.author: masn6811
insights.context: Analysis of unit matchups in a set with Tantalum Ray, Lucina, and Doomed Wall.

insights.insight: Tatsu Nullifier + Xaetron as a P2 combo is extremely favored and difficult to counter; most responses either lose to the combo directly or to Xaetron + single tech (Animus/Blastforge). P1 Tatsu rushes without Xaetron can be viable if the set offers strong red defense (Auric Impulse, no cheap red attackers).
insights.author: crash_overlord, mrguy888
insights.context: Extended analysis of Tatsu + Xaetron strength and counter-strategies across different unit sets.

insights.insight: Tatsu is effective against big attackers that you want to hold back to defend with. If your opponent gets 2 Tatsu units, large attackers like Colossus become significantly worse because you cannot hold them for defense.
insights.author: p0lari
insights.context: Discussion of why Colossus struggles against multiple Tatsu units.

insights.insight: Tatsu Nullifier is a hard counter to Plexo Cell, shutting down the opponent's best soak option, making it a key consideration in sets containing both units.
insights.author: supergreyman, cachotognax
insights.context: Discussion of unit interactions in a complex set with both Tatsu and Plexo.

insights.insight: Tatsu Nullifier is Not Viable (per viability criteria) in most base+1 sets because normal Fastimus Animus strategy is a defense that does not require Tatsu, and P1 cannot win symmetric B+1 via Tatsu-focused lines.
insights.author: consensus
insights.context: Formal viability classification of Tatsu Nullifier using established criteria.

insights.insight: P2 buying DD/DD as opening (turn 2) is a strong early commitment that can be punished; Tatsu requires opponent to play greedily without punishing early aggression to remain viable.
insights.author: awaclus, velizar_
insights.context: Analysis of Tatsu viability in different set contexts.

insights.insight: Tatsu Nullifier prevents the opponent from using cheap defense options effectively; when deciding whether to kill Tatsu early, the tradeoff is whether freezing it (denying its freeze effect) is worth more than using attack power elsewhere, and this is especially relevant in Iceblade games where Frostbite competes for freeze slots.
insights.author: .bky_1556
insights.context: Technical analysis of Tatsu freezing interactions, particularly with Iceblade units.

insights.insight: Quad-red units like Tatsu Nullifier, Lucent Hellion, and Apollo make sets feel faster than they appear; units like Shadowfang and Vai Mauronax can come from double Flame Animus but are not as inherently fast.
insights.author: mrguy888, darkeisbein
insights.context: Analysis of which advanced red units accelerate game pace.

insights.insight: Grimbotch and Tarsier should be mixed rather than played in pure form (Grimbotch+Grimbotch). Use pure Grimbotch when it's particularly inconvenient to defend or late game. Maintain some permanent attack via Tarsier so you have damage after Grimbotches timeout.
insights.author: .bky_1556
insights.context: Discussion about optimal unit mix in base+Grimbotch+Amporilla set.

insights.insight: Grimbotch is best held at 1 lifespan rather than clicked away, as holding provides more value. Attacking with Grimbotch costs 2 life to deal 1 damage; this is only worth it if you are breaching to kill a key unit like Shadowfang.
insights.author: itsotter
insights.context: Analysis of how to properly value and use Grimbotch in offensive and defensive contexts.

insights.insight: Grimbotch freeze decisions depend on absorber types: freezing 2LS Grimbotch with Vai is usually bad (loses tempo on offense), but can be correct if it forces opponent to sacrifice valuable defenders or blocks key unit interactions.
insights.author: apooche, .bky_1556
insights.context: Detailed analysis of freeze targeting strategy with Vai Mauronax.

insights.insight: Grimbotch is significantly higher value than Tarsier when it can deal full damage without absorb denial, forcing opponents to spend on soak, but only if the player switches to Grimbotches once opponent attackers threaten.
insights.author: Deleted User, swapgo
insights.context: Replay analysis discussing unit selection and timing between Tarsiers and Grimbotches.

insights.insight: Wincer is strongly enabled by Energy Matrix and Centurion (in that order), followed by Grenade Mech, Chrono Filter, and other burst units. Good absorb supports Wincer; good soak (especially delayed soak) weakens it. Wincer is positive-value even without firing if you have free mana, providing 15 health of defense worth more than the 5 drones spent over 2.5 turns.
insights.author: .bky_1556, p0lari, masn6811
insights.context: Discussion of Wincer synergies and value-holding mechanics.

insights.insight: Wincer is weak against Breachproof strategies because its damage is not efficient at breaking through burst absorb.
